data.title: Highlights of Berkshire Hathaway Annual Shareholders Meeting: The Successor's First Public "Stress Test"
data.description:
On Saturday, May 2nd, Central Time, the Berkshire Hathaway 2026 Annual Shareholders Meeting, a major annual event for the investment community, was held in Omaha. The meeting lasted approximately four and a half hours, with newly appointed CEO Greg Abel discussing several hot topics. Warren Buffett, at 95 years old, sat in the front row and spoke, while CNBC conducted an exclusive interview with Buffett. This was the first time in Buffett's 60-year tenure that Berkshire Hathaway had held its annual shareholders meeting "behind the scenes," and also the first public "stress test" for Buffett's successor, Greg Abel. Having the heads of Berkshire Hathaway's subsidiaries sit on the stage alongside Abel to answer questions was the most symbolic adjustment of this year's shareholders meeting. This move sent a signal to the outside world: Berkshire Hathaway's authority is no longer dependent on personal charisma, but will be built on a more diversified operating system. The key points of the shareholders' meeting summarized by Wall Street Insights are as follows: 1) Buffett on the market: This is not our ideal environment; people's gambling enthusiasm has never been higher. The most likely buying opportunity is when everyone else is not answering their phones. What people discuss and worry about usually doesn't happen; it's the sudden black swan event that shakes the market. 2) Buffett on his successor: Abel has done everything I've done before, and even more, and he's done it better in every way, so we give this decision a perfect score.

3) Buffett on Apple:

Ten years ago, I bought Apple stock for $35 billion, and including dividends, it's now worth $185 billion, and I did nothing.

Cook succeeded Steve Jobs and created one of the miracles of American business management.

4) Abel on AI:

AI must be beneficial to our business. We won't use AI for the sake of AI. We will deploy AI in a small-scale, value-creating way.

The fake Buffett video played at the meeting highlighted the cybersecurity risks brought by AI.

Data center construction and its demand on the power grid have brought huge growth opportunities to utility companies.

The energy usage costs of data centers should be isolated from those of grid users.

5) Abel on Investment: Reiterates that Apple, American Express, Moody's, and Coca-Cola are the "core four" cornerstones of the stock portfolio. He maintains "absolute cooperation" with Buffett on investment. Investments in Japan's five major trading companies are long-term and strategic; cooperation with companies such as Tokyo Shipping is being deepened. Berkshire Hathaway has a streamlined and efficient internal structure, possessing cross-group capital allocation capabilities; it will not split or divest subsidiaries. 6) Abel on Who His "Charlie Munger" Is: The partnership between Buffett and Munger is "unreplicable." I am surrounded by excellent people and have an excellent CEO team; I contact them to seek their advice. 7) Berkshire Hathaway Insurance Vice Chairman Ajit Jain: The Strait of Hormuz underwriting is "price-dependent," and US military escort is one of the prerequisites for underwriting this project. AI is unlikely to reach a point where it can weigh pricing, claims, and other aspects anytime soon; that will take many years. If you expect AI to tell you which stocks to buy and which to sell, I don't think that will happen. Earlier, Berkshire Hathaway also released its first-quarter financial report, some key points of which are as follows: Berkshire Hathaway's operating profit for the first quarter of 2026 was $11.346 billion, an increase of 18% year-over-year. Insurance underwriting profit increased by 28%, and profits from its railroad subsidiary BNSF increased by 13%, with a significant reversal in foreign exchange gains. Net investment losses narrowed to $1.24 billion from $5.038 billion in the same period last year, driving GAAP net profit up by approximately 120% year-over-year. Cash reserves reached a record high of $397 billion in the first quarter. As of March 31, 61% of Berkshire Hathaway's total fair value stock holdings were concentrated in American Express, Apple, Bank of America, Chevron, and Coca-Cola. The following is a transcript of the key points from the 2026 Berkshire Hathaway Annual Shareholders Meeting, in chronological order: The first half was co-chaired by Abel and Ajit Jain, Vice Chairman in charge of insurance. The second half was co-chaired by Abel, CEO of Burlington Northern Santa Fe Railway (BNSF), Katie Farmer, and CEO of NetJets, Adam Johnson. At the opening of the annual shareholders' meeting, Abel officially retired Warren Buffett's jersey bearing the number "60," permanently commemorating the "Oracle of Omaha's" decades of service with the company. Retiring a jersey is a tradition in sports and considered the highest honor bestowed upon an athlete. This jersey hangs high on the roof rafters, alongside the retired jersey of the late investment guru Charlie Munger—Munger's jersey bears the number "45," representing the number of years he served at the company. "I am pleased to announce that these two jerseys will hang there forever," Abel said. 21:20 Opening Remarks for the 2025 Berkshire Hathaway Annual Shareholders Meeting 95-year-old Warren Buffett was led to his seat in the front row of the boardroom, greeted with enthusiastic applause from the shareholders. This marks the first time in sixty years that Buffett has not been the absolute protagonist of Berkshire Hathaway's annual shareholders meeting. The Berkshire Hathaway shareholders meeting opened with a tribute to Buffett. A video montage reviewed precious photos and footage of the "Oracle of Omaha" over the years, accompanied by Hugh Lewis and The News Band's classic "Back in Time," interspersed with highlights from past shareholder meetings. Abel introduced key company personnel alphabetically, receiving enthusiastic applause when he introduced Buffett. At 21:45, Buffett praised Abel: "100% successful" CEO choice. Buffett took the microphone from his seat and again praised Abel. He pointed out that today was the anniversary of when he announced Abel as CEO. "This is the best decision we've ever made, 100% successful," Buffett said. "He did everything I've ever done, and even more. He's the right person." At 21:50, Buffett praised Apple CEO Tim Cook. In his opening remarks, Buffett asked outgoing Apple CEO Tim Cook to stand and acknowledge him, a statement that echoed the power transition within Berkshire Hathaway itself, from Buffett to the new CEO, Greg Abel. Buffett discussed the immense pressure Cook faced taking over from Apple founder Steve Jobs, and how he lived up to expectations and delivered outstanding results. Warren Buffett said: "Think about it, how much courage does it take to succeed Steve (Jobs) and surpass his achievements? It's one of the miracles in American business history. Thank you, Tim. After Steve's passing, our investment decision to bet nearly 10% of Berkshire's resources on Apple was effectively handed over to Tim, who turned that investment into a pre-tax return of approximately $185 billion." Tim Cook announced earlier this month that he would step down as CEO, with Apple's hardware chief John Turners succeeding him. At 10:00 PM, Abel explained the financial report. Abel stated that as competition intensifies, the insurance market is "easing." There has been an unprecedented trend of comparing prices among auto insurance customers. 22:20 Berkshire Hathaway CEO Abel Refuses to Follow the Trend and Bet on AI, Continuing Buffett's Investment Philosophy Berkshire Hathaway CEO Abel spoke about AI: "We won't use AI for the sake of AI. We will only invest when we see real value. AI must provide substantial benefits to our business. The application of AI brings opportunities to all businesses." Abel stated that Berkshire Hathaway takes a cautious stance on the application and management of artificial intelligence, a stark contrast to CEOs of other companies who are eager to reshape their business direction or rebrand around this technology. Abel stated that Berkshire Hathaway will deploy AI in a focused and value-creating manner, while also pointing out the potential risks this technology poses to "humans," which the company remains highly concerned about. 22:40 Abel believes data center construction will bring huge growth to utilities. Abel stated that the large-scale construction of data centers and the demand pressure they put on the power grid are creating considerable growth opportunities for the utility sector. Abel cited the expansion of hyperscale data centers in Iowa as an example, pointing out that current energy demand is still far from peak load capacity: Peak load for data centers—that is, actual electricity consumption—currently accounts for about 8%. Industry insiders generally hope to reach 5% to 10%, while we have already reached 8%. Therefore, we expect this proportion to grow by another 50%, or even more, in the next five years. Abel stated that isolating the electricity costs of data centers from those of ordinary grid users and ensuring that these costs are borne by the electricity-consuming companies themselves is crucial. "Hyperscale data center operators, data centers, and all types of electricity users—must bear all costs themselves." During the AI ​​boom, the pressure data centers put on regional power grids has become a focus of attention for many environmental and consumer advocacy groups. 22:50 Prefabricated Home Builder Clayton Hit by Interest Rates Abel stated that prefabricated home builder Clayton Homes is being hit by multiple pressures, including high mortgage rates, which are clearly driven by current interest rate levels. Consumers also face other challenges. Abel stated that the company's goal is to provide "affordable housing" for American consumers, a statement that drew enthusiastic applause from the audience. 23:05 The first question in the Q&A session was from Buffett: Why hold Berkshire Hathaway for the long term? Shareholders unexpectedly received a vivid lesson on the risks of artificial intelligence at this year's annual meeting. At the start of the Q&A session, Abel played a video featuring a familiar face. On the big screen, a man in a suit, posing as Warren Buffett, introduced himself and asked Abel: Why should investors hold Berkshire Hathaway stock for the long term? "Hello, my name is Warren, from Omaha. Abel, I've been following this company for a long time, a very long time. My question is simple. I'm 95 years old, and I have everything except time and cherry cola. I want to know—just so I can tell my shareholder friends—why they should hold Berkshire Hathaway stock for the long term?" Abel then revealed the truth: the video was not real footage, but a "deepfake" video generated using AI technology. He took this opportunity to emphasize cybersecurity risks to the shareholders present. Regarding the question from "AI Buffett"—"Why should investors continue to hold Berkshire Hathaway stock?"—Abel stressed the company's formidable strength in terms of its massive $397 billion cash reserves, which grants Berkshire considerable freedom of action. "We hold cash and U.S. Treasury bonds, which serve several purposes, and we do not intend to be subject to anyone," Abel reiterated the core investment and business philosophy long upheld by his predecessor, Warren Buffett. He told investors that holding cash in the form of U.S. Treasury bonds, maintaining financial independence, flexibly allocating capital, focusing on tax efficiency, and remaining highly vigilant against the “ABCs”—arrogance, bureaucracy, and complacency—remain Berkshire’s top priorities: “We’ve heard it countless times: the three poisons of arrogance, bureaucracy, and complacency can quietly erode a company and ultimately destroy it. We will never allow that to happen at Berkshire.” He described Berkshire as a unique enterprise—capable of integrating diverse businesses while possessing the ability to deploy capital quickly and flexibly: “Berkshire is a conglomerate, and we are keenly aware of that. But we are a different kind of conglomerate because we can allocate capital very efficiently.” We can transfer funds from insurance to non-insurance businesses, invest in the stock market, or hold cash when we deem it appropriate. Abel stated that this deepfake video of Buffett profoundly reveals the AI-driven cybersecurity risks Berkshire Hathaway is facing: This is a good wake-up call for our team. This is a significant risk that permeates Berkshire and that we deal with every day. Berkshire will commit to using technology to identify cyber threats, especially in the insurance business segment. Abel also specifically stated that the production of this deepfake video of Buffett was completely unauthorized and without the participation of the "Oracle of Omaha." 23:15 The first question in the real Q&A session: Considering current AI tools, where does human judgment still remain a competitive advantage for Berkshire? Vice Chairman of Insurance, Ajit Jain: Artificial intelligence is also very prevalent now. People are flocking to it in both the insurance and non-insurance sectors. Clearly, if artificial intelligence does become a reality as people expect, it will undoubtedly be a huge game-changer. Currently, we see AI being used as a productivity tool, a mechanism to reduce labor costs and perform routine, repetitive tasks. I don't think AI will be able to make decisions on things like pricing and claims that require trade-offs. That will take many years. Moreover, I tend to be skeptical. I would be surprised if someone told me they could solve that problem. So, if you're expecting AI to tell you which stocks to buy and which to sell, I don't think that will happen. Jain said that a few weeks ago, he was with Abel discussing this issue when Abel called his team and immediately brought up cyber risks, something they had already discussed. Then they quickly moved on to how we can improve the efficiency of writing and managing code, actually throughout the insurance business and in the building philosophy we focus on. They immediately brought that up. Then, as you mentioned, how to become more efficient. They also gave what I thought was a very good example. What I mean is, if we were looking at a risk, let a traditional underwriter do it, we might only focus on the five biggest risks, as your team pointed out. Now, we can be quite quick to see those big risks we're focusing on, but with technology we can also see other risks very quickly. We might be focusing on 15 other risks and have a strong judgment on them. 23:20 Question 2: How to balance patience and action? Question: As a young investor navigating uncertainty and rapid technological change, I often struggle to balance patience and action. How do you personally differentiate between the two? Answer: One of our greatest strengths at Berkshire is patience and discipline in allocating capital. Opportunities will always arise for you over time. This doesn't mean there aren't opportunities now, but it also doesn't mean you need to deploy all your capital or spend all your money right now. This is indeed our approach every day; we recognize that we hold a significant amount of cash and U.S. Treasury bonds—a crucial asset, for example. I would consider this cash holding an asset, a huge opportunity. You'll feel that moment when you sense an opportunity with a strong value proposition. When will we see those? We've outlined our investment philosophy, and a very important aspect of it is that we must thoroughly understand what we invest in. We want a deep understanding—you mentioned technology and the rapid pace of technological evolution and change you've seen. I always start from that point, and I know we've always done it at Berkshire: Do we understand the business? Do we understand the opportunity? More importantly, do we understand the risks? Then, we want to have a very clear view of the economic outlook for the next 5 to 10 years. Yes, next year is important, but we don't invest for the sake of investing for one year. We must have a long-term view of where the opportunities are headed. And we'll go even further—we will hold these investments forever. So we think like this: we want to have a strong impression of the management teams there, that they are capable and operate with high integrity. But most importantly, ultimately, the value must first justify deploying our capital. We are not in a hurry to invest capital in suboptimal opportunities. We want to know if it aligns with our principles, and then, as I said before, we will act decisively, quickly, and with substantial capital. 23:25 Question 3: Balancing Oversight of Wholly-Owned Subsidiaries and Stock Investments, How to View a Large Stock Portfolio? Question: Abel, given your background as a business operator, which differs from Warren's background as a public market investor, could you share how you balance your time between overseeing wholly-owned subsidiaries and the current $288 billion stock portfolio? Furthermore, compared to Warren's historical approach, does your operator's perspective change how you evaluate new investment opportunities? Abel shared new insights into how he views Berkshire's large stock portfolio, emphasizing a concentrated investment strategy anchored to a few core holdings. He refers to Apple, American Express, Moody's, and Coca-Cola as the "Core Four," considering them the cornerstones of Berkshire's stock investments. He also highlighted the company's large holdings in Japan's five major trading companies, listing them as another key pillar of the portfolio and emphasizing the long-term commitment to these companies. In addition to these core holdings, Abel also named several other significant investments, including Bank of America, Chevron, and Alphabet. Berkshire Hathaway purchased approximately $4 billion worth of Alphabet stock in the third quarter of 2025. Abel stated that he will play a more proactive role in investment management, adding to or adjusting holdings as needed. He added that he is "fully collaborating" with Buffett on investment decisions. Abel: "I have many years of experience running various businesses at Berkshire Energy, and then serving as vice chairman of non-insurance businesses. Fortunately, Jahn and I have held these great positions for the past eight years, and now nine. But this has created a very important opportunity for me to understand these businesses." As I've already mentioned, we have outstanding businesses and outstanding leadership, but there are still opportunities there. This does remind me that I'll be spending time on these businesses to ensure we're allocating capital appropriately, that we're still thinking about the risks in these businesses, and to encourage operational excellence. Because, listen, as an insider, it's easy to look at your internal metrics and convince yourself you're doing well; you have to look out and ask: What are our customers seeing and feeling? What are our competitors doing? I think that's exactly the value we can bring to our operations. I've mentioned giving Adam Wright more roles, or more responsibilities across 32 businesses. He'll bring excellent operational knowledge, and we also have the team on our insurance side. Now, let's talk about the equity portfolio and time allocation. There are still huge opportunities there when we're using the capital on our balance sheet. I shared our cash and U.S. Treasury holdings. I want to emphasize that if you look at our current equity portfolio, as I explained in the letter, we have a concentrated portfolio. We emphasize this by calling it the core, but the best way to put it is that it is indeed a concentrated portfolio. We have what we call core and concentrated investments. I highlighted our Japan investments in the letter. Interestingly, if you look at some of the companies where we hold very important positions, I'd like to add that for those companies, we may still be buying stocks or rationalizing appropriate positions in our portfolio. So the first group, which I emphasized, is slightly under $200 billion and remains at that level. We now have close to $100 billion, or $85 billion. Then add Berkshire's other investments, such as Bank of America, Chevron, Google, etc., and that's another $70 billion invested. This highlights that a very large portion of our total investments is highly concentrated in a limited portfolio, and active management of those investments is actually limited, which is what I really want to emphasize. We also understand those businesses. We understand the management teams. These are things that Warren and I will still absolutely work together and discuss. You don't need to discuss them every day, but if something goes wrong in these businesses, we'll discuss it in that week or month. Maybe it's about their direction, or what we've learned. The Japanese company just released its results in the last 48 hours, which is a hot topic of discussion; Warren and I discussed their results and what we've seen there yesterday morning. So, these are core, but that doesn't mean we're putting them aside, or that they're just concentrated investments we keep an eye on as we evaluate them. Ted manages another $20 billion, or slightly less, in capital, but his responsibilities go far beyond that. He's obviously helping us with a variety of other opportunities, or helping us assess risk or capital allocation in our businesses. So we're fortunate to have these, but when you consider the management and the amount of work required around it, it's a very manageable portfolio. As we've already mentioned, the opportunity to deploy this cash and U.S. Treasuries at the right time is a very significant one, including equities, including what we might see in our operating businesses, and also in insurance. Regarding time allocation, yes, we will spend a certain amount of time on operations, and we will prioritize that because we see a huge opportunity to continue improving and closing the gap in operational excellence. We see opportunities in our existing portfolio, but this is either about increasing holdings or scaling up. Then we continuously evaluate what other opportunities exist in the market, whether it's a full acquisition of a private or public company. Similarly, we are also considering what incremental opportunities exist if we were to own a partial stake in a company. These opportunities are evaluated in the same way, as I said, we look at the economic outlook. And it's closely related to the previous answer. Jain: I really think capital allocation and operating a business are two sides of the same coin. There's something Warren said years ago that I think makes a lot of sense. He said: A good capital allocator will be a good operations manager, and vice versa. Abel: When you think about our operating company, as I mentioned before, we have a very deep talent pool. We have excellent operators who understand their businesses. They understand their industry, their customers. Yes, is there still room for improvement? Yes, it's a continuous improvement process, and we will close those gaps. But we have an exceptional team out there. Whether it's Jain, myself, or Adam Wright, we take the time to make sure we're happy with how we allocate our capital, we understand the risks, and then we're aware of those gaps.

23:35 Question 4: Patience Has an Opportunity Cost, How Do Long-Term Investors Think About Capital Allocation?

Question: When patience has a real opportunity cost, how should long-term investors think about their capital allocation approach today? How can individuals balance patience with action, especially given that Mr. Buffett's decades-long track record has shaped the standard?

Abel: Again, regarding our capital allocation approach and the long-term approach we take, it's very much in line with our owners and the shareholders here today. They take a very long-term approach to investing. We are fortunate to have this unique owner base in our holdings. And, in the long run, Berkshire will have significant opportunities.

This brings us back to patience and discipline in capital allocation. Do we know what will happen tomorrow? Or will that event happen in three or two years? But market misalignments will occur, which will again allow us to act. This is where our disciplined approach comes in—knowing what our investment philosophy is around these activities. It's not that we don't see excellent companies today. We'd love to own many. I'll be careful. In the long term, we'd be happy to hold those companies because there are excellent companies with great management teams, and we evaluate them. I would say that when you think about the world, it doesn't mean there are dozens of such companies, but they exist. But we're not interested in acquiring those companies at that price relative to the opportunity, the company's economic prospects, and the associated risks—whether it's a partial or full stake. This doesn't mean that the opportunity won't come in the future. This is what we spend time preparing for: first, maintaining discipline; second, recognizing some core opportunities that we value or value at the right price. It really does come back to discipline. You asked me how I personally balance patience and action. Again, it's consistent with my role in this company. I'm fortunate to work with Warren, Jain, and others because we do this because we love and believe in Berkshire. Warren brings a huge commitment to Berkshire, a deep understanding and passion for it. Based on that, he wants to create something very long-term, including the opportunities it might create. Personally, and I know all of us, we bring the same passion, and we are fully committed to doing this in a way consistent with the past. Jain: You know, insurance, like investing, is a game of patience. It's very difficult to get people to stand idly by and do nothing. When I recruit people, my usual approach is to tell them directly. I say, your job is to say "no." You'll be bombarded with deals day after day, but your fundamental responsibility is to say "no." I said, occasionally you'll come across a deal that's like being hit by a plank, it'll scream "Money's here!" Then you come back to me, and we'll decide together whether to do it or not. You know, just kidding, it's really hard to sit there doing nothing while everyone else is being swayed by brokers and taken to London. I think the real test of success in the insurance industry, and certainly in the investment world, is the ability to say "no." 23:40 Question 5: Providing Insurance for Ships Crossing the Strait of Hormuz When asked when and how Berkshire Hathaway provides insurance for ships crossing the war-torn Strait of Hormuz, Ajit Jain, vice chairman of Berkshire's insurance business, gave a concise answer: "Simply put, it depends on the price." Before he finished speaking, laughter and applause erupted in the room. Jahn stated that Berkshire Hathaway is involved in a program to insure ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz, but no policies have been signed yet. The Strait of Hormuz has been closed or heavily controlled multiple times during the wars between the US, Israel, and Iran. "We have participated on a small scale in a program aimed at insuring ships in the Strait of Hormuz, but no policies have been signed yet," Jahn said. He added that escorting ships through the Strait by the US Navy will be one of the conditions for coverage. "The plan is still being refined. But if we can secure satisfactory terms—including conditions at the underwriting decision-making level and the guarantee of US Navy escort—we have offered an underwriting price that we believe is acceptable. However, there has been no substantial progress yet." 23:45 Question 6: How to manage the investment portfolio built by Warren Buffett? Abel: Regarding managing the existing portfolio and its contents, as you mentioned, it was built by Warren, but it's a group of companies Warren knows very well. And I'm very confident that I understand these businesses and their economic prospects. So, that's why when I outlined this in the letter, I really wanted to convey a message: Yes, we are very happy with these companies, we understand them, and yes, it's a concentrated portfolio, but you know, their businesses will evolve, and there may be risks. So we will continue to evaluate it, but it's a portfolio we are very happy with. Warren has mentioned Tim Cook's phenomenal success at Apple. Warren and Tim recently discussed this, and they said that Warren's investment in Apple wasn't because it's a tech stock. He saw what the product was and how much individual consumers valued it. That's an extraordinary perspective, but it's also a very similar perspective that I think many of us would apply. For example, in the power business, I know a lot; I know how to ensure power generation, how to transmit it, and so on. But am I really that interested in how Apple phones are made? I'd be curious about where they're made and some of the risks and challenges surrounding that. But I have complete trust in our team when we discuss it on a broader scale. We examine ourselves and ask: Do we understand its value and why this product is valuable? That's really its value to consumers. I think we have a unique opportunity, and we're very fortunate that Warren comes to the office every day. We're lucky enough to discuss other potential opportunities that might exist, bringing different skill sets. But ultimately, we quickly narrow it down to what the opportunity is, why it's valuable, why consumers, or users in any industry, why that company and that product will last. Then, related to that, are the associated risks. That's basically Warren's approach, and it's mine too. Regarding our existing portfolio, we're always clear about what we've invested in. But in terms of understanding the opportunities and risks involved, we're very confident we have a clear view of it, and we're happy with where we are. 23:50 Question 7: Jahn and Succession Planning for the Insurance Business, Abel's Succession Plan When asked about Jahn and his successor plan, Abel stated that the board takes such matters very seriously: "They have developed a plan and are continuously discussing it. So, if Jahn is unable to fulfill his responsibilities today, or if I am unable to fulfill my responsibilities, our board knows exactly what action to take." These two succession plans are clearly important topics. Jahn joined Berkshire in 1986 and is the architect of our insurance business. We created an unparalleled franchise with an exceptional culture and discipline. When Warren announced the transition plan last year, the first thing he did was gather our top five insurance managers to sit down and discuss the business and the culture. This was an exceptional opportunity for me to expand my knowledge base in insurance. What I saw in that team was very deep management and insurance experience, and they shared the same values ​​and culture that Jain emphasized. Maintaining a disciplined culture is challenging. In the insurance industry, telling an underwriter who's used to being active to "take a few months off" isn't easy. But Jain had an excellent team around him, and our board took succession very seriously. We had a plan in place so that if Jain or I couldn't fulfill my responsibilities, the board knew what to do. Regarding culture and underwriting orientation, I follow some simple rules. Very few people are actually involved in decision-making; my top three have been together for over 35 years. Compensation plans are fixed salaries, not complex formulas that allow individuals to gain upside while Berkshire bears downside risk. We shield them from market fluctuations so they can focus on doing the right thing. I've seen all these compensation plans over the years. I once told Warren: Give me a compensation plan, and I can exploit loopholes in it, and you won't find out for years. Add to that the fact that employees who lose want to renegotiate, while those who win are happy to take everything and leave. It's a huge challenge.

23:55 Question 8: When will Berkshire's utilities phase out fossil fuels?

Question: When will Berkshire's utilities phase out fossil fuels, switch to renewable energy alternatives, and stop causing irreparable damage to the environment and the future of my generation?

Abel: We operate as stewards of these assets, serving our states and customers. First and foremost, we absolutely need to comply with existing laws, including federal law. Our team is committed to both compliance and doing things right. We have plans regarding resources and when to phase out coal-fired and gas-fired units, which are largely driven by state policies. State governments will decide how we operate and how long these assets operate, because ultimately, it is the customers who bear the costs and risks.

Look at our Iowa utility; about 93% of our energy comes from renewable sources, leading the nation, and at an affordable cost. But we still operate coal-fired power plants, which we need to stabilize the system during peak periods, and we won't use them unless absolutely necessary. The challenge is that hyperscale data centers put a lot of stress on the system. If artificial intelligence continues to develop, the number of carbon-based units used will increase, which will put a lot of stress on the system and the entire industry. 01:20 Abel Returns to Stage to Host Afternoon Session of the Annual Shareholders Meeting Greg Abel returned to the stage at the CHI Health Center in Omaha, Nebraska, to host the afternoon session of the Berkshire Hathaway Annual Shareholders Meeting. Accompanying Abel were BNSF Railroad CEO Katie Farmer and NetJets CEO, President of Consumer Products Services and Retail, Adam Johnson.

01:25 Question 9: How does geopolitics affect Berkshire subsidiaries?

Question: How has the current geopolitical situation in the Middle East affected Berkshire's subsidiaries?

Abel: It certainly affects all of our businesses in many ways. But what I'm most proud of is that we operate these businesses with a long-term perspective. When the phone rings, you know there will be some challenges, but that's okay. We'll discuss it, we'll work hard, and we'll always find a way to overcome them. As for the situation related to the war with Iran and the conflicts in the Middle East, I see the team again taking this attitude: this is the reality we face. What is the best solution for our customers? How can we continue to serve them as we have in the past?

I mentioned LSBI Pipeline's drag reducers. They don't usually sell large quantities of products to the Middle East, but when they start figuring out how to address this challenge, a lot happens. This doesn't mean our business isn't directly affected.

Our chemical group's input costs actually doubled in a very short period. Over time, prices will rise according to our contracts, and this situation will rebalance. As far as running our business is concerned, we really just work hard and sustain everything in the long run. BNSF CEO: Railroads are really a good reflection of the state of the industrial and consumer economy because our loads cover a wide range of commodities. We've seen several different impacts from the Middle East conflict. Due to supply chain disruptions, it created opportunities for some of these commodities; demand for commodities like aggregates and steel is increasing. The largest part of our business is intermodal transport, and as fuel prices rise, our intermodal business becomes more competitive. But overall, if fuel prices remain too high for a prolonged period, it will affect consumer demand, impacting all of our businesses. Yes, we've seen some impact. Some large retailers say that consumers are now having to make choices, to choose what to buy. If the environment of high fuel prices persists for a long time, I do believe we will see this customer impact ripple through our business. NetJets CEO and President of Consumer Products Services and Retail, Adam Johnson, stated that rising costs, including oil prices that once climbed to $100 per barrel, have begun to suppress demand in some areas: "In consumer products and brick-and-mortar retail, this has indeed affected some demand." While acknowledging these pressures, Johnson stated that the company's businesses are accustomed to finding solutions amidst volatility. "We are prepared to deal with these situations and make adjustments as necessary. But this is indeed affecting some retail and consumer products businesses." 01:35 Question 10: How is Berkshire Hathaway's decentralized model managed? How does BNSF maintain its competitiveness? Question: Berkshire Hathaway's system relies on decentralization. Each manager, as CEO, manages their own subsidiary. Which operating units require more oversight, and how are underperforming managers handled? How will BNSF maintain its competitive edge relative to competitors and new technologies, given that its profitability lags behind its rivals? Abel: I emphasized decentralization, risk discipline, and capital allocation. We have a group of outstanding leaders and businesses that are closest to their customers, and if they think like owners, we will achieve very good results throughout the group. But decentralization doesn't mean we shirk responsibility. This autonomy means accepting it readily, which comes with a huge sense of responsibility and pride in doing things well. We have many expectations—are they managing risk? Do they see themselves as chief risk officers? Are they good at allocating the capital at hand? If we see poor performance or some bad decisions, that's when we step in and discuss it. BNSF CEO: We fully understand that continuing to drive efficient operations, maintaining a competitive cost structure, and continuing to close the profitability gap with our competitors are crucial. The first thing we're really focused on in 2025 is improving locomotive operational efficiency. Improving the locomotive network frees up resources, creates capacity, and allows you to handle the same or even more freight volumes with fewer assets. In the first quarter of this year, we handled more freight than in the first quarter of last year, but we used 260 fewer locomotives. The second area is around our technology transformation. We're bringing in data scientists and operations research researchers, putting them alongside our operations staff in network operations centers, researching digital twins, and providing predictive ETAs for our customers. We achieved record fuel efficiency in the first quarter. Regarding competition with trucks, we have the largest intermodal network of all rail companies. We used to have five people operating one train; now most of our trains only have two. But we also need to be allowed to innovate and need regulations that support rails competing with trucks. NetJets CEO: I came back on June 1, 2015. I asked a question: How many people truly understand both ends of our business? NetJets is complex; we fly to thousands of airports in 150 countries. I didn't like the answer; it was far too few. We started rebuilding the culture from there. I remember preparing for my first board meeting, and I was talking about growth. Abel kindly pulled me aside and said, "Why don't you let Warren worry less and focus on reducing the debt first?" That was a lesson I've learned. We talked about security and service. Warren acquired NetJets in 1998 after becoming a customer, and he said, "I want security, I want service." We've always been very focused on making sure everyone is on that track. That's largely why we've been able to pay off our debt, return cash to Berkshire Hathaway, and become a leader in the service industry.

01:50 Question 11: The Impact of Tariffs on the Portfolio?

Question: Is Berkshire Hathaway considering seeking tariff relief or compensation programs for its wholly-owned operations facing import costs? How significant is this impact across the entire portfolio?

Abel: The impact of tariffs on our entire portfolio is very similar to the discussion surrounding the Middle East situation. We experienced it once during our first term in government and learned from it, so we are better prepared. That is, to keep a close eye on things and manage them ourselves. We will find ways to continue serving our customers and recover these tariffs through contracts directly with them or through the products we are producing. Our team has done an excellent job in handling this issue. There are many things to clarify at the moment, and we are not actively seeking these.

BNSF CEO: No, not in terms of compensation, but I would like to say a few words about the impact of tariffs.

BNSF CEO: No, not in terms of compensation, but I would like to say a few words about the impact of tariffs.

In early 2025, we saw some customers ship ahead of schedule before the tariffs took effect, resulting in increased freight volumes. This then stabilized in the second half of 2025, and by 2026, our customers had indeed adapted to and adjusted to the tariffs. That said, it did introduce some uncertainty. From a planning perspective, this was very difficult for our customers, causing some capital to hold back on investing in manufacturing facilities. It is precisely this tariff uncertainty that we are truly seeing the impact on our customers. NetJets CEO: I would use Berkshire Hathaway Automotive as an example; its new car sales this year are slightly lower than last year, partly due to the impact of tariffs. The problem is that tariffs change every day, and simply understanding this "bouncing ball" of tariffs is a task in itself. Among the 32 consumer goods, services, and retail companies in our portfolio, their average age is 88 years. When I call those CEOs, they say, “We’ve been dealing with tariffs for 100 years.” Think about the CEOs of the last seven or eight years—we’ve had to deal with the global pandemic, the highest inflation in forty years, and now these “bouncing ball” tariffs. Businesses have done an excellent job dealing with these issues, and I think we’re in a pretty good position for the future.

01:55 Question 12: Japanese Investment Portfolio

Question: Berkshire’s investments in five Japanese trading companies are passive, good businesses purchased at good prices and financed in yen. Your deal with Tokyo Marine is completely different; it’s a ten-year joint acquisition and reinsurance partnership. This is a level of operational integration that Berkshire has never attempted internationally before. What does this look like in practice? Does this foreshadow a shift towards more proactive international partnerships under your leadership?

Abel: Tokyo Marine has done an excellent job. I set the stage earlier by saying it was a strategic relationship, not a financial transaction.

We love our 2.5% investment in Tokyo Marine Transport; it will be a long-term investment. It's the same type of investment we have with five others in Japan—we truly believe they are forever because it transcends the investment itself and is more about the relationship we hope to build there. You'll continue to see this, as detailed in the underwriting opportunities where we co-invest in their risks and returns, effectively representing 2.5% of what's currently on their books. Again, it's part of a financial transaction, but it also involves a tremendous amount of trust. The third thing we're talking about is that the partnership emphasizes various things, and how we hope this relationship will develop is not yet clear. So we'll continue to let it develop naturally. This partnership shares the same culture and values ​​as us. So there's no doubt it will be excellent for many years to come. As for seeking absolute acquisitions in insurance or other areas, that will evolve over time, and obviously that's something that Jahn and the Tokyo Marine Transport management team will discuss. We would be very happy if such an opportunity arises.

02:00 Question 13: Will Berkshire Hathaway Divest Businesses or Be Broken Up?

Question: Are there any future scenarios in which you can foresee Berkshire Hathaway divesting businesses or being broken up? If so, what are they?

In response to the above questions from shareholders, Abel stated that he does not expect Berkshire Hathaway to break up or divest its subsidiaries. He emphasized the lack of bureaucratic layers in Berkshire's structure and the group's unique ability to flexibly allocate capital across its various business segments. "We are a conglomerate, but we are a highly efficient conglomerate. We do not have layers of management."

Abel stated that Berkshire is committed to holding its acquired companies for the long term, but may have to consider selling them in certain situations.

“When we buy something, we hold it permanently. When we acquire a utility company, we tell the regulators it's a permanent holding. But it has to be a workable relationship. If the relationship breaks down, we look for a better way out.” Abel stated that intractable labor disputes or reputational risks could prompt Berkshire to divest a business. Nevertheless, Abel concluded, “We don’t consider divesting subsidiaries or splitting up the group.” When we think about this, in some cases, we might not be the best owner of a business. If there are labor issues we can’t resolve, or reputational risks we don’t want Berkshire to face, then that company doesn’t belong to the Berkshire family. If a business is unsustainable and no longer generates operating cash flow for our shareholders, and if someone else can run it and make it more successful, then we have to consider that. We take the obligation to ensure that capital is properly allocated very seriously. We have actually announced the sale of Pacific Corporation's utilities in Washington state. In Washington state, they wanted Pacific Corporation to implement policies that had a significant impact on costs in our other states. Our other states were bearing costs imposed by another state, so we opted to exit and found a very good buyer. When we buy something, we always treat it as a "hold forever" deal, but it has to be a viable relationship, and if that relationship breaks down, we'll find a better path. Regarding the second part of the question, there will absolutely be no breakup. We are a conglomerate, but we are an efficient conglomerate with no layers of management and no bunch of committees telling us how our business should operate. Many conglomerates end up with layers upon layers of costs that don't add value to the company as a whole, but we won't do that. Our conglomerate structure operates without bureaucracy and bloated costs, allowing for very tax-efficient capital transfers between different groups. We will not divest subsidiaries or break up any groups as a result.

02:10 Question 14: Safety First or Seizing More Investment Opportunities? Preferring Tech Companies or Cash Flow Companies?

Question: Compared to Warren, what is the most significant evolution in your personal framework for assessing cash flow certainty and margin of safety? Specifically, do you prefer tech companies that demonstrate equally strong cash flow?

Abel: Regarding how Warren views the investment approach, our so-called margin of safety for investments, and how we handle it, we are absolutely aligned. It stems from our culture and values, and the way we've handled everything over the years.

If I go back to examining opportunities in the energy sector, it quickly turns to: Do ​​we really understand the risks associated with it? At the time, we were acquiring Nevada Energy, and I had three major risks clearly in mind that I was eager to discuss with Warren. Our immediate conversation was: fully understanding the economics, and then moving directly to the biggest risks. One of those risks was rooftop solar, and how it would disrupt the business.

That risk did surface 12 months and 18 months later, and we dealt with it successfully. We think about risks differently; we look at them the Berkshire way, looking ten years ahead: What will this business look like in ten years? If we don't understand what it will look like in ten years, then we don't do it. We must have a vision of what the future will look like; that's the core of our approach. Now, when we talk about tech companies, we never say that a particular industry is something we absolutely must participate in. If there's a company in the tech industry that we understand its opportunities and risks, and its valuation is reasonable, then just because it belongs to the tech industry doesn't exclude us from that possibility. 02:15 Question 15: Who is Abel's "Charlie Munger"? Question: Warren has had Charlie's partnership for most of his time as CEO, which naturally reduces the risk of us making wrong investment decisions. Who will be Abel's Charlie? When asked who would be his "Charlie Munger," new CEO Greg Abel didn't name anyone, but instead spoke of the entire team surrounding him. "You surround yourself with great people, and they're already here," Abel said. He mentioned Adam Johnson—President of Berkshire Hathaway's Consumer Products, Services, and Retail businesses and CEO of NetJets—as well as Ajit Jain, Vice Chairman of the Insurance business, and Katie Farmer, CEO of BNSF Railroad. All three executives appeared alongside Abel on Saturday. He said, "We are very fortunate to have a group of outstanding people in our CEO team, and I will proactively reach out to any one of them, regardless of the specific situation, to get their input." Abel added, "We are very fortunate to still have Warren as our Chairman, which has facilitated a smooth transition. We have a very good board of directors, and I can easily reach out to any of them, depending on the specific situation." When I answered Warren's question in Omaha, I said we want Berkshire to endure. I want to lead Berkshire, and I will be a strong leader. But you have to surround yourself with great people, and they're already here. In non-insurance businesses, I've been fortunate to work with the 32 companies Adam manages, and 18 others. Obviously, I have an excellent working relationship with Jahn and am fortunate to frequently seek his advice. Then there are our CEOs; we are fortunate to have such a great group of people, and I will contact any of them on specific situations to get their opinions. Fortunately, because of Berkshire and the way we were created, we have an extremely rich pool of resources around us. Berkshire will endure, and it will endure as a team.

data.title: Can prediction markets win the race for perpetual contracts?
data.description:
Over the past year, we've spent considerable time reporting on perpetual futures (PERS) trading platforms. Their rapid rise is undeniable. Perpetual futures allow participants to price events shortly after they occur, offering high leverage and ample liquidity around the clock. Existing exchanges have never offered this service due to limitations in trading hours and days. An 11-member team has built Hyperliquid into the fastest-growing cryptocurrency exchange with nearly $1 billion in annual revenue thanks to this 24/7 trading concept.

In 2025, perpetual futures trading volume averaged seven times that of spot trading. This seemed like a reliable path to building a sustainable business. So, inevitably, others followed suit.

Last week, two major prediction markets, Polymarket and Kalshi, announced the launch of perpetual futures and cryptocurrency trading within hours of each other. And just a few months ago, Hyperliquid also announced the launch of event contracts.

The convergence of perpetual contracts and prediction market platforms seems like a natural progression. Everyone wants to be an all-encompassing exchange, offering a one-stop shop for attention, capital, and leverage. Three weeks ago, Saurabh wrote in a report on X that Hyperliquid's foray into prediction markets would help the exchange dominate the financial arena. But does the reverse hold true? Will Polymarket and Kalshi's moves yield similar returns? Today, I'll tell you all about it. Why Perpetual Contracts Are Important for Prediction Markets Prediction markets suffer from stickiness. They are typically cyclical, with trading volumes reaching all-time highs when there are major events to bet on—like during US presidential elections, the Super Bowl season, or Federal Open Market Committee meetings. During the US presidential election in November 2024, Polymarket's monthly active users peaked at 321,500. Three weeks later, this number dropped by 25% to 245,000. However, the number of monthly users fluctuates due to seasonal factors. In January 2025, Polymarket's user base peaked at 500,000, before falling below 200,000 in September. This reflects Polymarket's user retention rate. Dune's user base data shows that since 2024, only 8% to 11% of users have remained active in the market a year after joining. Approximately 75% of users churn within 90 days. Users may return to participate in events, but this doesn't necessarily translate to a sense of engagement with the platform.

But this is only part of the problem.

Prediction markets also freeze funds until the problem is resolved. Perpetual contracts, on the other hand, update event prices every second, thus attracting attention for a longer period and building continuous user interaction. This is also more advantageous for prediction markets because traders have larger trading volumes and higher commission revenue.

In 2025, the nominal trading volume of malicious traders exceeded $60 trillion, while the nominal trading volume of precious metals traders was $28 billion.

In 2025, the nominal trading volume of malicious traders exceeded $60 trillion, while the nominal trading volume of precious metals traders was $28 billion.

Therefore, this expansion into adjacent domains of prediction markets (PMs) becomes a natural evolution. Platforms that cater to certain speculative needs often expand their business into other areas. They either develop the relevant functionality themselves or acquire other platforms that possess that functionality. We have witnessed this many times: Robinhood expanded from the stock market to the options market, the cryptocurrency market, and finally into prediction markets (PMs). Coinbase acquired Deribit for a record $2.9 billion, entering the derivatives trading space. Binance also expanded from offering spot trading to futures trading, eventually creating its own native blockchain. We often see this in traditional sectors. A company expands its services, hoping to cross-sell new products to the same group of customers. This serves two purposes: first, to increase average revenue per user (ARPU), and second, to diversify reliance on multiple revenue streams, thereby enhancing the company's resilience to market cycle fluctuations. In the early 1970s, commodity futures revenue at the Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) was declining. So, they used a former 4,000-square-foot smoking lounge at their parent company, CBOT, to create the Chicago Board Options Exchange (now Cboe). Because both required shared infrastructure, they were able to work together: risk management, clearing, and a network of professionals knowledgeable in derivatives pricing. However, there's a huge gap between wanting to operate a perpetual contract trading platform and actually having the ability to implement it. Operating a perpetual trading platform involves many aspects. Let's start with liquidity. The Hyperliquid platform processes over 200,000 orders per second through a fully on-chain order book. This exchange settles over $6-7 billion in trading volume daily, using a two-sided market-making model. Insufficient liquidity can lead to extreme volatility, excessive bid-ask spreads, and high slippage, making it easier for whales to manipulate prices. Secondly, there's the risk engine—the core of any derivatives platform. It tracks every trade and checks the margin requirements for each order. In October 2025, the cryptocurrency market lost $19 billion, and the Hyperliquid platform processed billions of dollars in liquidations without interrupting service. Additionally, there's the funding rate mechanism, which links a trader's price to the spot price of the underlying asset. This mechanism operates continuously by settling small amounts between long and short positions every few hours. Building the entire technology stack isn't the main problem; I believe prediction markets can do that. The bigger problem lies in stress testing this technology stack. Hyperliquid built all of these systems and stress-tested them in real-world scenarios, such as a 10/10 cryptocurrency liquidation event and the Iraq War. Afterward, once the entire system was ready, it launched event contracts via HIP-4. Kalshi and Polymarket, on the other hand, are trying to do the opposite. They operate successful prediction markets that don't require any of the systems mentioned above. Now, they not only have to compete with the highly successful Hyperliquid, but also with a system that hasn't been stress-tested and can't handle the high-frequency activity of perpetual trading, thus vying for market share. For prediction markets, there are many headwinds that make expanding to perpetual trading more difficult than the other way around. Hedging Synergies On the Hyperliquid platform, the Risk Engine monitors all your positions across all trading instruments, spot, and upcoming event contracts. Saurabh explains this in his HIP-4 report. It looks at all your positions, indiscriminately. Ultimately, the leverage you use and the margin you hold as cross-collateral determine when you will be forced to liquidate. The combination of your positions in spot, futures, prediction markets, or any other market determines how much margin you need to hold. But Saurabh, aren't other blockchains like Ethereum or Solana also composable? Absolutely. On a universal chain, each application runs its own risk engine within its own smart contract. They cannot atomically see each other's state. Therefore, Kamino cannot know what's happening on Pacifica. Aave cannot know what's happening on Lighter. All applications are smart contracts on their own chains. Each application or smart contract has an independent risk engine, and to make them aware of each other—that is, to create a universal risk engine—requires massive collaboration. This universal risk engine solves a core funding problem by optimizing the same funds across multiple trades made by a trader on a trading platform. Let's say a trader is going long ETH on the Hyperliquid platform with 5x leverage. She's worried about the Federal Reserve's interest rate decision next week, so she buys a "Fed Keeps Interest Rates Unchanged" outcome contract at $0.65. Because the same risk engine is used, both positions are held in the same margin account. If the Federal Reserve unexpectedly cuts interest rates and the price of ETH rises, her long position profits, while the loss in the outcome contract is only her initial investment. If the Federal Reserve keeps interest rates unchanged, the outcome contract will pay out gains, partially offsetting the loss on her long position. This is why prediction market platforms or hedging trading venues cannot simply be add-ons. This hedging possibility is precisely the value of HIP-4 on the Hyperliquid platform. Ordinary traders on the platform view prediction markets as insurance against the reversal of their existing hedging positions. Currently, collateral on the Polymarket and Kalshi platforms will be locked until the event is resolved. Therefore, unless they provide a unified risk engine between their real-money trading and prediction market trading venues, they will lose a key factor that keeps traders on the platform. Currently, neither of these platforms has announced a cross-margin system between their prediction market trading and real-money trading venues. The segmentation of prediction markets and the average trader profile have further fueled concerns about their ability to replicate their success in real-money trading. Over 80% of Kalshi's total monthly trading volume comes from sports betting. Polymarket's figure is projected to exceed 40% by 2025. So how can a sustainable pricing mechanism be built around these sporting events for paid trading platforms? This would exclude a significant portion of traders from paid trading. Furthermore, Kalshi's average traders are retail investors with no prior cryptocurrency experience, funding their prediction market accounts via ACH transfers from bank accounts. Therefore, even assuming the theoretical possibility of cross-margining on the Kalshi platform, I doubt whether these traders possess the expertise required to double down on their investments and utilize perpetual contracts as a hedging strategy. What approaches might be effective for prediction markets? If Kalshi and Polymarket announce cross-margining, I believe there's one scenario where these bets would work. Their institutional partnerships with major brokers and clearinghouses could facilitate high-value, high-frequency trading activity in event contracts and perpetual futures. This would allow institutional trading desks to view prediction markets as part of a broader risk management toolkit. Both Kalshi and Polymarket have partnerships that allow them to reach institutional clients. Kalshi's partnerships with FIS and Tradeweb data, and Polymarket's deal with the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), could help retain institutional clients who value using perpetual contracts to hedge their prediction market positions on the same platform. This remains a distant goal, requiring many factors in the prediction market to move in a favorable direction. They need to build stress-tested infrastructure, forge partnerships, and demonstrate to customers that their platform can help optimize capital allocation. But these are essential for their survival in this fiercely competitive market. With distribution channels already dominated by Hyperliquid, they have no choice but to seek maximum opportunities elsewhere.

data.title: Robots Land in Vietnam: Is AI the Next Big Thing in Southeast Asia?
data.description:
While everyone is still focused on Indonesian e-commerce, Thai tourism, and Singaporean finance, a group of Chinese AI entrepreneurs have quietly turned their attention to—Vietnam. Today's Vietnam is strikingly similar to China five years ago—high GDP growth, a massive influx of foreign investment, and a labor dividend that, while still present, has reached a turning point. The difference is that this time the story isn't about "moving factories in," but about "making factories smarter." From the robot baristas at Hanoi's "Coffee Shop of the Future" to the humanoid robots soon to be deployed at the Foxconn factory in Bac Ninh province, Vietnam's robot revolution has quietly begun. I. Why Vietnam? In 2025, Vietnam's GDP growth rate reached 8.02%, ranking among the top in Asia, and its per capita GDP exceeded US$5,000, successfully entering the ranks of middle-to-high-income countries. In the first quarter of 2026, the GDP growth rate was still as high as 7.83%, and registered FDI reached US$15.2 billion, a year-on-year increase of 42.9%, of which manufacturing accounted for more than 70% of FDI. Vietnam's population has exceeded 100 million, with young workers accounting for more than 60% and an average age of less than 33. But a problem has arisen—more and more factories are being built, but there are not enough young people. A contradiction of mixed feelings is forming: on the one hand, the manufacturing industry is growing larger and larger, and foreign investment is pouring in like crazy; on the other hand, labor costs are rising continuously, and young people are unwilling to "work in factories to tighten screws." This gap between "strong demand" and "insufficient supply" is precisely the fertile ground for the most natural "essential need" for robots. The Vietnamese government has also recognized this. By the end of 2025, Vietnam officially listed Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) as one of the six priority strategic technology products, paving the way for the robotics industry at the national strategic level. II. What businesses can robots do in Vietnam? ① Warehousing and Logistics Robots – The Most Certain "Billion-Dollar Track" Vietnamese e-commerce is booming. The e-commerce market size reached $15.3 billion in 2025, with parcel volume continuing to climb at an annual growth rate of nearly 30%. However, a key statistic reveals a huge market gap: Vietnam's warehouse automation penetration rate is only 2-3%, while the global average is close to 10%. This means there is still 3-5 times the potential for growth. AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots), as core equipment for flexible automation, are moving from the "proof-of-concept" stage to the "large-scale deployment" stage. Market research institutions predict that Vietnam's warehouse automation market will continue to maintain double-digit growth. Leading players have already established successful models. Viettel Post's automated sorting center has achieved a daily processing capacity of 1.4 million parcels—the technical feasibility has been proven; the remaining task is simply to scale up replication. ② Industrial Quality Inspection and Intelligent Manufacturing – AI is Transforming Production Lines The Vietnamese manufacturing AI market is projected to grow from $1.15 billion in 2025 to $4.8 billion in 2031, representing a CAGR of 26.6%. This is not just empty talk. In March 2026, Maihe Robotics exported a complete automotive welding production line to Vietnam, marking the "overall export" of Chinese intelligent equipment to Vietnamese factories. Hongban Technology invested $110 million in Ninh Binh province to build an intelligent manufacturing base, fully introducing world-class automated production lines. Yadea completed its first phase of intelligent factory construction in Bac Ninh province, Vietnam, with an investment exceeding $100 million and an annual production capacity of one million electric vehicles. Samsung, Foxconn, Luxshare Precision, and other giants have already heavily invested in northern Vietnam. These high-standard factories are forcing the entire upstream supply chain to upgrade towards automation—quality inspection robots, collaborative robots, and AGVs are becoming standard equipment in foreign-invested factories. Service and Humanoid Robots—From "Novelty" to "Essential Need" Hanoi's "Coffee Shop of the Future" is already a hit—four robots act as baristas, waiters, calligraphers, and photographers, working 6-8 hours a day without interruption, with young consumers lining up to experience it. In Ho Chi Minh City, robots are already helping to deliver documents in government service centers, acting as guides at exhibitions, and mixing drinks at hotel bars. An even stronger signal comes from Foxconn. In April 2026, a Foxconn subsidiary submitted an environmental impact assessment report to the Vietnamese government, planning to produce humanoid industrial robots at its factory in Bac Ninh province, with official production commencing in November 2026. The global manufacturing giant is building a "humanoid robot production line" in Vietnam—the significance of this signal is self-evident. Vietnam is leveraging its "latecomer advantage," skipping the purely mechanical stage and directly entering the AI-driven intelligent robot market. This path is progressing faster than expected. III. Who is quietly making these moves? Chinese companies have already established a presence in Vietnam. Aishida announced a 150 million RMB investment in Vietnam to build production lines for cookware, small appliances, and industrial robots, stating that this "is beneficial for the company's rapid strategic deployment of industrial robots in Vietnam and the Southeast Asian market." Moji Robotics signed a strategic cooperation agreement with Vietnam's Geleximco Group to comprehensively promote robot R&D, manufacturing, and application in Vietnam. Sophis Intelligent showcased its flexible robot technology at the Vietnam Industrial Exhibition, signing a Vietnamese distributor agreement on-site. More than 50 Vietnamese heavy industry companies have expressed their need for transformation.

· Vietnam's local strength: More than just Chinese players

· VinRobotics & VinMotionVinRobotics, a subsidiary of Vingroup, Vietnam's largest private enterprise, was established at the end of 2024 and produced a robot prototype from scratch within seven months.

【Vietnam's local strength: More than just Chinese players】

If Chinese companies are the "beachhead raiders," then Vietnamese local giants have begun to "fight on their home turf," and they are acting swiftly and with great determination.

· VinRobotics & VinMotion: A subsidiary of Vingroup, Vietnam's largest private enterprise, it was only established at the end of 2024 and produced a robot prototype from scratch within seven months.

The products are already in use at the VinFast factory and are working closely with Qualcomm, directly targeting the top-tier international market. VinDynamics: Also part of Vingroup, focusing on humanoid robot R&D, it recently partnered with German parts giant Schaeffler to accelerate its global expansion. CT Group: A Vietnamese conglomerate with a market capitalization of 250 billion VND, it previously secured an order to export 5,000 drones to South Korea, pursuing a dual-track strategy of drones and robots. Menas Group: It has deployed Unitree Robotics' MEME and NANA robot dogs in high-end shopping malls in Vietnam, using service robots to optimize customer experience; the speed of commercialization is astonishing. Furthermore, the American robotics company AMC Robotics has also established a factory in Ho Chi Minh City in January 2026 to produce industrial quadruped robots, making Vietnam its Asian production hub. International giants are simultaneously increasing their investment. Qualcomm has just announced the 28 shortlisted startups for its 2026 Vietnam Innovation Challenge, with AI and robotics being the core tracks. Ho Chi Minh City has established an initial 500 billion VND (approximately US$20 million) technology venture capital fund, specifically focusing on robotics and automation. Capital and industry are accelerating simultaneously. From Chinese entrepreneurs to Vietnamese giants, and then to international supply chains—three forces are simultaneously investing in Vietnam. This is not a bubble, but a structural industrial transfer. IV. Risks? Of course there are. No market is without its challenges. Vietnam also faces several hurdles: • Incomplete industrial chain: core components rely on imports, and the local supply chain is not yet mature. High after-sales costs: Local robot and automation engineers are scarce, requiring maintenance teams to be built from scratch. • Tiered willingness to pay: Large clients (such as Samsung and Foxconn) have ample budgets, but small and medium-sized factories are accustomed to a "try before you buy" approach. • Power bottleneck: Vietnam's peak electricity consumption in 2025 is projected to increase by 11.1% year-on-year, and fluctuations in energy supply may affect the operation of automated equipment. However, these challenges are precisely the barriers to entry—whoever establishes a successful localized service system first builds a competitive advantage. V. Conclusion: How Long is the Window of Opportunity? If the story of Vietnam over the past 10 years was "from an agricultural country to the world's factory," then the story of the next 10 years will undoubtedly be "factories transforming themselves into smart factories." Robots are no longer a question of "whether or not to come," but rather "whether arriving late will lead to missed opportunities." When AI transforms from a concept into a machine capable of working continuously for 16 hours in a humid and hot workshop—the gold rush map of Southeast Asia will be completely redrawn. The window is still there, but it won't stay open forever.

data.title: Why do investors from all over the world flock to Buffett's Omaha meeting?
data.description:
With the Omaha Shareholders Meeting on May 2, 2026, drawing ever closer, my friend Leo (a close friend who translates Warren Buffett's letter to shareholders with Lao Wang) is both researching travel plans and answering fans' questions: "Why is Berkshire Hathaway's shareholders meeting so special? It lasts all day, with shopping and airplane viewing—it's like a carnival!"

That's certainly a good question. Today's shareholders meeting format isn't just something casually arranged. It actually reflects Buffett's unique ideas about corporate governance, shareholder relations, and capital culture.

Let's travel back in time and see how this gentleman, step by step, transformed a dry, legally necessary procedure into "the Woodstock of the capitalists."

First Step

Escaping Mondays: When "Shareholders' Choice" Wins Over the Chairman

Now, shareholder meetings are fixed on Saturdays, which seems natural. But initially, this was even against Buffett's personal wishes.

In 2002, the new Qwest Center in Omaha was completed, with ample parking, and Buffett, for the first time, gave shareholders the choice of meeting date. He stated explicitly: "I want to hold it on Mondays." The reason was practical: weekdays were more convenient for him and his team.

However, shareholder democracy gave a completely different answer.

According to the 2003 letter, "We won on Saturday by a 2:1 vote." Buffett, true to his word, added humorously, "At Berkshire, shareholder democracy will take some time to return." This had a significant impact. Holding meetings on Saturdays was essentially for the convenience of shareholders, not management. It allowed commuters to attend and greatly alleviated traffic and parking headaches (a point Buffett mentioned many times later). This established the tone: the shareholders' meeting was first and foremost a holiday for shareholders, and only secondarily a formality for the company.

Step Two

Disrupt the Agenda, Relegate the "Boring Parts"

An even more drastic change came in 2004. Buffett performed a major overhaul on the traditional shareholder meeting process.

Previously, formal shareholder proposals and voting took center stage. But Buffett discovered that a few proposers "raised topics that were of little interest to most of the audience, wasting a lot of time—they must have enjoyed speaking to a roughly 19,500-strong audience." (2004 Letter to Shareholders)

His solution was simple, brutal, and practical: reverse the order of the agenda.

Since 2004, the process has changed: the doors open at 7:00 AM, a movie starts at 8:30 AM, and a Q&A session that lasts for several hours, beginning at 9:30 AM and continuing until 3:00 PM. Only then does the formal meeting take place briefly. Buffett explained, "This way, shareholders who want to attend the full meeting can stay, and those who aren't interested can leave early, or better yet, go shopping." The essence of this change is two words: **respect**. Respecting the value of shareholders' time. The most informative Q&A sessions are placed during prime time, while legally required formalities are postponed. It assumes that shareholders are there to learn and exchange ideas, not to fulfill tedious obligations. Meanwhile, the phrase "or better yet, go shopping" unabashedly treated shareholders as customers and business partners, pioneering a new approach that blended "exhibitions" and "conferences." The third step: Embracing technology and using live streaming to resolve the double awkwardness. In 2015, Buffett accomplished another major feat: broadcasting the annual shareholders' meeting globally for the first time. His reasoning in the letter was so candid it was almost laughable: "There's really no other way: it might reduce the number of people coming. Last year, over 40,000 people came, the pressure is immense." Facing reality: "The second reason is even more important. Charlie is 92, and I'm 85… Shareholders shouldn't have to travel all the way to Omaha just to see how we look and sound. (Please be kind with your comments, after all, even at our best, we weren't exactly pleasing to the eye.)" This candor is invaluable. It not only solved the problem of insufficient seating but also, in a humorous and direct way, responded to everyone's whispers about "whether the old men are still up to par." The online livestream didn't diminish the value of the in-person event; instead, it served as a global invitation, expanding Berkshire Hathaway's community culture and making the experience of physically attending Omaha even cooler.

Step Four

Activating the Showroom, Bringing the Shopping Center into the Convention Center

From initially urging attendees to "bring your money" at the Nebraska Furniture Market to later creating a nearly 200,000-square-foot showroom in the convention center, shopping has always been an indispensable part of the shareholders' meeting. This isn't just simple sales; Buffett understands human nature perfectly, achieving three goals at once: Creating a hands-on experience: Letting shareholders see and touch Berkshire Hathaway's products (from See's Candies to Clayton mobile homes) transforms the abstract "investment portfolio" into a concrete, engaging consumer experience. Shareholders instantly become customers, boosting their sense of connection. Promoting subsidiaries: This is the perfect stage for subsidiaries to showcase their strength to their most important and friendly customers. CEOs can chat directly with shareholders. Providing an interesting place to go: As mentioned earlier, it provides a fantastic place for shareholders who aren't interested in formal processes. The whole event is therefore very flexible, ensuring everyone can have fun. The shareholders' meeting thus became a closed-loop ecosystem: morning Q&A sessions absorbed ideas, afternoon shopping released energy, and shareholders seamlessly switched between the roles of "boss," "student," and "shopaholic." The meeting was like a closed loop: shareholders absorbed ideas through Q&A in the morning and released energy through shopping in the afternoon, seamlessly switching between roles as "boss," "student," and "shopaholic." Step Five: Optimizing Q&A, from "Seating Frenzy" to "Fair Lottery" With more and more people attending, figuring out how to ask questions became a challenge. There were even instances of "a 100-meter sprint" as the meeting opened at 7 am, with everyone frantically scrambling for microphone positions. In 2008, Buffett had to be honest: "From a security perspective, this won't work, and we don't think that those who are quick enough should have the right to ask questions." So, he introduced a lottery system. Starting in 2009, at 8:15 AM on the day of the meeting, a lottery was held in front of each microphone to determine who would ask a question.
