At the same time, the sources of questions became more diverse, with some coming from shareholders selected in the audience and others from submissions screened by journalists, ensuring that the questions were both informative and varied. This change reflects Buffett's commitment to "fairness" and "quality." It avoided a scramble for attention, gave every attending shareholder an equal opportunity, and made the Q&A session more substantial and engaging. Looking at these changes together, Buffett's design logic couldn't be clearer: Shareholders are the main players: prioritize comfort and efficiency. Extreme pragmatism: avoid formalism and dare to overturn old rules for truly valuable things. Speak your mind: don't shy away from problems (such as age or large numbers), use humor and practical actions to alleviate concerns. Build an ecosystem: upgrade meetings into super parties that integrate ideas, business, and culture. What he orchestrated wasn't just a meeting, but a large, tangible party for a highly trusting, interactive, and engaging shareholder family. Here, shareholders didn't feel aloof respect, but rather a warm sense of belonging.

data.title: The farce of a 20-year-old internet celebrity becoming a father has turned a multi-million dollar bet on Polymarket into a joke.
data.description:
Author: Mahe, Foresight News

On Polymarket, everything is bet. On May 1st, the prediction market surrounding "whether Clavicular will become a father in 2026" had accumulated over $14 million in trading volume, with approximately $4.5 million traded in the past 24 hours.

The screenshot shows that the market was created on April 20, 2026. This isn't ordinary sports or election betting; it's an extreme bet surrounding the personal life of a 20-year-old Kick anchor—whether he will make a "credible" announcement of becoming a father by December 31, 2026.

... The rules are clearly stated: only credible statements from Clavicular himself or his representatives are valid; jokes or unreliable content are invalid; the announcement date is the determining factor, regardless of the child's actual birth date. However, the protagonist merely announced his girlfriend's pregnancy during a live stream, and Polymarket, after multiple rounds of controversy and voting, ruled it "Yes." This quickly sparked widespread online discussion and debate, primarily concerning two aspects: first, the sensitivity of the topic itself—is turning a social media influencer's "birth plan" into a multi-million dollar bet overstepping boundaries? Second, the fairness of the oracle settlement mechanism. The UMA decentralized oracle repeatedly proposed "Yes," leading to controversial voting, with the final token-weighted result overwhelming the majority of individual voters, resulting in a deluge of accusations of "manipulation" in the comments section. When large investors are both bettors and voters, can the truth still prevail?

20-year-old internet celebrity who once sparked huge controversy

Clavicular's real name is Braden Eric Peters. At only 20 years old, he grew up in an ordinary middle-class family; his father was a businessman, and his mother was a bodybuilding enthusiast. He rose to fame through live streaming on the Kick platform and short videos on TikTok.

Clavicular's tagline is "looksmaxxing"—an extreme subculture of physical enhancement that emphasizes "hardcore enhancement" of attractiveness through drugs, surgery, and lifestyle choices.

Clavicular's tagline is "looksmaxxing"—a subculture of extreme physical enhancement that emphasizes "hardcore enhancement" of attractiveness through drugs, surgery, and lifestyle choices.

He began injecting testosterone and other controlled substances at age 14, publicly discussing the anorexic effects of bone marrow removal, bimaxillary osteotomy, liposuction injections, steroids, peptides, and methamphetamine, even calling himself a "lab guinea pig" in the looksmaxxing community. His username, "Clavicular," is derived from his collarbone width—he claimed his shoulders were 19.5 inches wide, pursuing a golden ratio face and physique. His content primarily targeted young male viewers, promoting the idea that "appearance equals power." From 2025 onwards, his exposure increased exponentially. He walked the runway at New York Fashion Week, gave interviews to mainstream media outlets such as the New York Times and GQ, but also frequently became embroiled in scandals: in March 2026, he was arrested for assault; in mid-April, he allegedly overdosed on drugs during a live stream and collapsed, later being hospitalized. Several of his YouTube channels were permanently banned for violations (promoting controlled substances, harmful content targeting teenagers). In April 2026, he proposed a "concubine-selection" fertility plan: during a live stream, he announced a "dating show" similar to a beauty pageant, recruiting 500 women to participate, with the winner becoming the mother of his child. He publicly stated, "I must have a child, I want a child today," and "I will become a father by the end of next year," and even shared his "fertility stack." These remarks quickly sparked a strong backlash on social media, accused of objectifying women. It was against this backdrop that Polymarket launched on April 20th, with traders generally believing he "would definitely announce it." Shortly after the market's launch, Clavicular's live stream again generated buzz. Recently, during a live stream, he emotionally claimed that he was pregnant with a child from a girlfriend he claimed to be 18 years old (whom he had only known for 10 days), breaking down in tears and emotionally. This clip quickly went viral, with related Kick clips garnering tens of millions of views. However, the facts were swiftly reversed. Multiple investigations revealed that the girlfriend had broken up with him and was not pregnant; the entire incident lacked any medical documentation, doctor's statement, or official announcement, and biologically, confirming and publicly announcing a pregnancy within 10 days is highly improbable. Clavicular himself did not subsequently issue a formal, credible statement, and the entire process resembled his usual chaotic live stream style. Despite this, Polymarket Yes's share increased instead of decreasing. Trading volume surged to $4.5 million in a single day during the controversy, with some large investors placing heavy bets. UMA Oracle Voting Manipulated Again According to Polymarket rules and the UMA oracle mechanism, settlement is phased: proposers first submit Yes/No, which can be challenged within the dispute window. If it enters the DVM (Data Verification Mechanism), UMA token holders vote. A minimum of 65% approval and 5 million UMA tokens from participants are required for approval. This market experienced two cycles of "proposed Yes → dispute," ultimately settling on Yes. According to the comments section below the Polymarket market, the controversy lies in the voting result: almost every long-time Polymarket user believes it shouldn't be considered Yes because it's premature and doesn't meet the judgment rules. Even some Yes holders share this opinion. However, in the token-weighted vote, Yes won with 67%. Key figures included UMA Rocks—the largest voting group of Polymarket traders who also held market positions. Polymarket player Domahhhh commented that member "Scout" was one of the largest Yes holders and a key member of UMA Rocks. Scout had been banned from Polymarket's Discord for suspected criminal activity before this incident. Scout then pushed UMA Rocks to officially vote Yes. The second-largest voter (a Risk Labs employee) immediately switched to Yes after the UMA Rocks vote. This was for self-preservation; if you voted against UMA, you would lose money. In the first round of voting, Yes led by a narrow margin because the vast majority of token holders voted Too Early, but UMA Rocks, along with a few large whales, voted for Yes. Due to UMA's mechanism—which encourages the most popular answer rather than the truth—any option that led in the previous round is highly likely to ultimately win. The vote now overwhelmingly favors Yes. Currently, Scout has been removed from UMA Rocks. Polymarket has not yet responded to this event, and players who bet on NO have already lost their chips.

data.title: Structural Cleansing in the Crypto Industry Amid Geopolitical Conflicts and Liquidity Retreat
data.description:
Preface

In the first quarter of 2026, global financial markets entered the most severe risk repricing cycle since 2022. The continued escalation of geopolitical conflicts, the full-scale return of trade protectionism, and the repeated swings in monetary policies of major economies collectively created a highly uncertain macroeconomic environment with low liquidity preferences. The crypto market suffered a systemic sell-off during this quarter, with Bitcoin plummeting from approximately $93,000 at the beginning of the year to the $63,000 range, a maximum drawdown of over 38%; the altcoin market was even more devastated, with many tokens falling 60%–80% from their cycle highs.

This deterioration in the market environment was not accidental, but rather the result of multiple structural pressures. On the one hand, the Trump administration's aggressive tariff policies and the US-Iran military conflict pushed geopolitical risks to new highs, putting pressure on risk assets across the board; on the other hand, the crypto market's own narrative fatigue and the fragility of its liquidity structure were fully exposed during the downward trend.

Bitcoin's narrative as digital gold failed to deliver on its safe-haven function in a wartime environment, instead exhibiting itself as a high-beta risk asset; altcoins, lacking new capital and fundamental support, have fallen into a continuous value regression. Meanwhile, the rapid evolution of AI is profoundly reshaping the intersection of technology and finance. The continuous iteration of large-scale model capabilities, the explosive growth of open-source AI agent frameworks, and the initial integration of AI with crypto payment scenarios provide noteworthy new variables for the industry's medium- to long-term development. The following analysis will dissect the core variables of the first quarter from four dimensions: the macro environment, Bitcoin and capital structure, AI development trends, and the differentiation of the altcoin market, and provide ArkStream Capital's framework judgment on future trends. Macroeconomic Environment: Geopolitical Conflicts and Trade Wars Constitute Systemic Pressure The fragmentation of the global trade system is accelerating. The primary analytical framework for understanding first-quarter policy actions is the US midterm elections in November 2026. The Republican Party's current slim majority in both the House and Senate faces a severe challenge; historically, the ruling party has almost inevitably lost seats in midterm elections. Under this pressure, the Trump administration's series of actions in the first quarter—including tough tariffs, the Greenland sovereignty narrative, and military strikes against Iran—essentially serve the same political logic: consolidating the Republican base through aggressive foreign and economic policies and creating a narrative of a "strong president" in an election year. This means that, at least before the November vote, the unpredictability and aggressiveness of policies are unlikely to cool down substantially, and the political uncertainty premium faced by risk assets will persist throughout the year. Driven by this political logic, the global trade order experienced its most severe shock since the establishment of the WTO in the first quarter. In mid-January, the Trump administration announced tiered tariffs of 10%–25% on eight countries: Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands, and Finland, using the Greenland sovereignty dispute as leverage. At the end of January, a bill imposing 500% punitive tariffs on countries purchasing Russian oil (directly targeting BRICS countries such as China, India, and Brazil) gained bipartisan support and entered the voting process. On February 20, after the U.S. Supreme Court rejected the legal basis for tariffs under the International Economic Partnership Agreement (IEEPA), Trump immediately invoked Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 to reimpose a 10% global provisional tariff (valid for 150 days). The repeated escalations and changes in tariff policies impact the market from three dimensions: On the cost side, import tariffs directly drive up commodity prices and inflation expectations, limiting the Federal Reserve's room for interest rate cuts; on the supply chain side, retaliatory measures from trading partners exacerbate global supply chain uncertainty, suppressing companies' willingness to invest in capital; and on the risk premium side, the unpredictability of the policies themselves becomes the biggest pricing challenge, systematically increasing the implied volatility of various risky assets. ArkStream Capital believes that the latter two effects are far more difficult to quantify and fully price in the market than the tariff rates themselves.

? [Chart Placeholder] Screenshots or maps related to the Greenland sovereignty dispute

https://truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump/posts/115925897257210763

The essential difference between this round of trade conflict and previous rounds of tariff negotiations lies in the fact that the conflict has expanded from bilateral to multilateral, and the repeated changes in the legal basis indicate that policy-making itself has deviated from the institutionalized track and entered a new stage of "immediate administrative power." The suppressive effect on risky assets far exceeds the direct economic impact of the tariff rate itself.

The Tail Risks of the US-Iran Military Conflict On February 28th, the US and Israel launched a coordinated military strike against Iran, causing oil prices to jump within hours and global financial markets to enter risk-averse mode. With traditional markets closed for the weekend, the cryptocurrency market, as the only major asset class trading 24/7, bore the brunt of the risk-averse selling pressure. Bitcoin fell from $65,500 to $63,000 within an hour, and over $515 million in leveraged positions were liquidated. This event once again confirms the fact that, under the current institutional participation structure, crypto assets play more of a "liquidity relief valve" role than a safe-haven tool in extreme risk events. When traditional markets are closed, the cryptocurrency market becomes the only operational channel for global funds to release risk, and its price behavior reflects liquidity structure more than fundamental logic. The impact of the shock extends far beyond the price level. Within hours of the strike, the Iranian leadership announced the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, one of the most vital arteries in global oil shipping, through which nearly 20% of the world's maritime oil trade passes. This massive tail risk superimposes the energy supply shock onto already high global inflation and directly impacts European and Asian economies heavily reliant on imported solar power. Even if a ceasefire is reached through negotiations within days, the pricing of tail risks is not completely eliminated. If the conflict escalates again, the market reaction could be far greater than the initial shock. For the crypto market, this shock carries two pressures: first, rising oil prices push up inflation expectations, further compressing the Fed's room for interest rate cuts; second, investors' pre-market anxieties can only be released through the crypto market. Meanwhile, the uncertain future situation in Iran is also a core macroeconomic variable for geopolitical risk signals in the second quarter. If the conflict escalates further under the political logic of a midterm election year, the suppression of risk assets will not be a repeat of the first quarter. The Fed: The fluctuating path of interest rate cuts and the delay in the liquidity inflection point. Macroeconomically, BTC, as a peripheral market in the financial industry, has always had a significant correlation with the Fed's interest rate policy.

Federal Reserve Interest Rates vs BTC Price (2022–2026)

Since 2022, there have been approximately four distinct cycles.

2022-2023: Interest Rate Hike Cycle → BTC Under Pressure

The Federal Reserve began aggressively raising interest rates at the beginning of 2022, from near zero to 5.5%. During the same period, BTC plummeted from approximately $47K to $16K. High interest rates increased the opportunity cost of holding a non-yielding, risky asset like BTC, leading to capital outflows.

Second half of 2023 - early 2024: Pause in interest rate hikes → BTC bottoms out and rebounds

While interest rates remained in the 5.25%-5.50% range, the market began pricing in expectations of interest rate cuts. The price of BTC rebounded from $16K to over $60K. Meanwhile, in January 2024, the SEC approved 11 spot BTC ETFs, marking BTC's official entry into the mainstream financial system.

September 2024 - October 2025: Interest rate cut cycle → BTC surge

The Federal Reserve cut interest rates for the first time by 50 basis points in September 2024, followed by three consecutive rate cuts, totaling 100 basis points within the year.

Three interest rate cuts were completed in 2025, totaling 1.75%. During this period, BTC surged from $66K to an all-time high of $128,198. The Federal Reserve maintained interest rates at 3.50%-3.75% at its January and March 2026 meetings, citing rising oil prices pushing up inflation expectations. Both PCE and core PCE forecasts were revised upwards to 2.7%. Coupled with tensions in the Middle East, BTC fell from $126K to approximately $74K currently. Interest rate cuts reduce the opportunity cost of holding BTC while increasing market liquidity, historically strongly correlated with BTC price increases. The Fed's dot plot indicates an expected rate cut of one time in 2026, which is not good news for the price of BTC. The Fed's policy path underwent a gradual hawkish shift in the first quarter, with rate cut expectations gradually compressed from two at the beginning of the year to near zero. The FOMC meeting on January 28th maintained the federal funds rate at 3.5%–3.75%, marking the first pause after three consecutive rate cuts in 2025. In his post-meeting statement, Powell reiterated that "more data is needed to confirm a sustained decline in inflation," a cautious but relatively neutral tone. However, the meeting minutes released on February 18 revealed a much deeper internal division than stated in the statement. The minutes showed that several officials explicitly mentioned the possibility of an interest rate hike, the first time such wording appeared in the minutes since 2023, and market confidence in interest rate cuts began to crack. On January 30, Trump announced the nomination of former Federal Reserve Governor Kevin Warsh to succeed Powell as chairman (the nomination was formally submitted to the Senate on March 4), with an expected inauguration in May. Warsh served as a Federal Reserve Governor from 2006 to 2011, and was one of the youngest and most hawkish members of the committee, having resigned in opposition to a second round of quantitative easing. It's worth noting that his public statements have softened somewhat in recent years, arguing that AI-driven productivity gains have a structural deflationary effect, thus supporting lower interest rates. However, his most prominent label remains "balance sheet reduction over interest rate cuts." Warsh has repeatedly criticized the Federal Reserve for maintaining an excessively large balance sheet, blurring the boundaries between monetary and fiscal policy, advocating for a significant reduction in the balance sheet before discussing interest rate cuts; even if nominal interest rates decline slightly, the liquidity tightening resulting from aggressive balance sheet reduction could offset the effect of interest rate cuts (some analysts estimate that every $1 trillion reduction in the balance sheet is roughly equivalent to a 50bp interest rate hike). The market's interpretation of his nomination is generally hawkish, with some institutions viewing it as "an intriguing choice for a president who wants low interest rates." Following the nomination announcement, precious metals fell first (gold fell 1.9% in a single week), the dollar strengthened, and the market began to reprice what a Fed chaired by this type means. Entering February, the situation became further complicated. On February 28, the US and Israel launched a military strike against Iran (see Section 1.2 for details), causing oil prices to jump above $100 per barrel, completely altering the inflationary landscape. Cost-based inflation driven by tariffs had not yet been digested, and the supply-side shock to energy prices compounded the situation, creating double inflationary pressure. The policy space for interest rate cuts was severely compressed, and the market began to seriously discuss a previously absurd possibility: the next step would not be a rate cut, but a rate hike. The FOMC meeting on March 18 put all of this into writing. The meeting maintained the interest rate unchanged by a vote of 11:1 (the January meeting was 10:2, with Miran and Waller opposing; in March, Waller switched to favor, with only Miran opposing – a shift itself a noteworthy signal of hawkish restraint). The post-meeting statement specifically added the phrase "the impact of developments in the Middle East on the US economy remains uncertain," formally introducing the war factor into the Fed's decision-making framework. The updated Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) and dot plot released clear hawkish signals: the 2026 inflation forecast was revised upward to 2.7%, and the median rate cut in the dot plot remained unchanged at one this year (consistent with December, Powell explicitly stated that "the median has not changed"), but 14 of the 19 committee members expect only 0–1 rate cuts this year, a clear hawkish shift in distribution; CME FedWatch shows the market has compressed its expectations for rate cuts this year from two at the beginning of the year to a maximum of one, with the possibility of zero rate cuts significantly increasing. The unemployment rate forecast remained at 4.4%, and GDP growth was slightly revised upward, showing an overall nascent form of stagflation characterized by resilient growth and sticky inflation. Bitcoin responded by falling from $74,000 to $70,900, with ETFs experiencing a net outflow of $129 million that day. Some market analysts pointed out that Warsh's first move after taking office might not be a rate cut, but a rate hike. Looking back at the first quarter, the collapse of interest rate cut expectations was not an event, but a process. From the interest rate hike discussions emerging in the January minutes, to the hawkish personnel expectations brought about by Warsh's nomination, to the second wave of inflation caused by the Iran war, and then to the March dot plot compressing the year's interest rate cuts to one, the market's initial expectation of two rate cuts was stripped away layer by layer, and the narrative of "Higher for Longer" once again took the lead. Previously, the market generally believed that "the interest rate inflection point has been established, and the liquidity inflection point is only a matter of time," but the evolution in the first quarter gave a more extreme answer. Not only is the liquidity inflection point still far away, but the interest rate inflection point itself is also at risk of being overturned. If Warsh's proposed combination of interest rate cuts and balance sheet reduction is implemented, it means that even if nominal interest rates decline, the actual liquidity environment may be tighter, and the suppressive effect on risk assets should not be underestimated. Against this macroeconomic backdrop, global risk assets are under pressure across the board: the S&P 500 has fallen more than 5% year-to-date, and the Nasdaq has fallen even more; gold ETFs have absorbed more than $16 billion in funds; Bitcoin ETFs saw a net outflow of $3.8 billion in February alone. The signal of funds migrating from digital gold to physical gold is extremely clear. ArkStream Capital will continue to monitor the evolution of interest rate cut expectations and geopolitical situations, as these are the core variables for judging the timing of subsequent liquidity inflection points. Bitcoin and Fund Structure: Narrative Falsification, ETF Differentiation and Institutionalization Process Digital Gold Stress Test One of the most noteworthy structural changes in the first quarter was the significantly enhanced correlation between Bitcoin and the Nasdaq index during stress periods. In market stress tests, Bitcoin not only failed to provide a hedging function, but also showed a downward trend almost synchronously with technology stocks. From a behavioral finance perspective, this is not surprising. Since the approval of ETFs, institutional investors have included Bitcoin in the same risk budget framework as technology growth stocks. When macro risk appetite contracts, deleveraging and reducing positions are systemic and not differentiated based on asset class. This surge in correlation marks a new stage in Bitcoin's institutionalization process: it is evolving from a standalone alternative asset into a high-beta subset of global risk asset portfolios. The first quarter data thoroughly exposed the limitations of the digital gold narrative: gold exhibited two distinct trends in the first quarter: it reached a historical high of approximately $5,280 per ounce in early January, but with rising expectations of a hawkish Fed and increasing real interest rates, a significant pullback occurred in the latter half of Q1, with a maximum drawdown of nearly 20%; while Bitcoin fell by more than 20% throughout the quarter; between November 2025 and January 2026, the Bitcoin ETF... The cumulative net outflow reached approximately $6.18 billion, marking the longest continuous outflow period in history, while gold ETFs recorded a net inflow of over $16 billion during the same period. The signal of funds migrating from digital gold to physical gold is extremely clear. The digital gold narrative surrounding BTC has become ineffective. Through data comparison, we found that BTC shows a high correlation with Nasdaq, but a very low correlation with gold.

BTC vs Nasdaq / Gold Rolling Correlation (2020–2026)

Normalized Price Increase Comparison — BTC / Nasdaq / Gold (From 2022)

BTC vs Nasdaq: Highly Correlated, But Diverging

Before 2020, the 60-day rolling correlation between BTC and Nasdaq was generally around -0.2.

Comparison of Strategy (MSTR) stock price and BTC price movement

The landmark event that brought this round of pressure on the DAT strategy was the continued deep losses after Bitcoin fell below Strategy's holding cost line in Q1. As the world's largest publicly traded Bitcoin treasury in terms of holdings, Strategy held approximately 674,000 BTC at the beginning of the quarter and continued to increase its holdings throughout the first quarter, reaching approximately 762,000 BTC by March 24; according to SEC 8-K disclosure data, the weighted average holding cost at that time was approximately $75,694 per BTC (total cost of approximately $57.69 billion).

... This means that Strategy's unrealized loss threshold was around $76,000 for Bitcoin. When Bitcoin plummeted from $88,000 in early January and fell below $76,000, Strategy was already experiencing unrealized losses, not, as some reports claimed, "only starting to lose money at the end of February." The low of around $63,000 at the end of February meant that the unrealized loss depth was once close to 17%. This has strong symbolic significance in market psychology: if even the most steadfast and largest institutional holders are deeply trapped, the confidence of retail investors and small and medium-sized institutions is even more fragile. More alarmingly, a large portion of Strategy's Bitcoin purchases came from convertible bonds and preferred stock financing. If the stock price remains depressed, preventing the conversion conditions from being triggered, the pressure to repay principal at maturity could force the company to sell BTC on the secondary market, creating a death spiral of "price decline → financing difficulties → forced selling → further price decline." However, in March, Strategy displayed a stance completely contrary to market panic, continuously and massively increasing its BTC holdings at low prices through its preferred stock instrument STRC (Strife Preferred Stock), further averaging down its holding costs by taking advantage of the low prices during the market panic. This operation, on the one hand, reflects the Saylor team's firm belief in the long-term value of Bitcoin, and on the other hand, objectively provides some buying support to the market. But there is another side to the coin. The continued increase in holdings further increased Strategy's leverage exposure and debt size. If the price of Bitcoin continues to fall, the pressure on its balance sheet will be amplified non-linearly. This makes Strategy both an anchor of market confidence and an amplifier of potential systemic risks. As of the end of the first quarter, although Strategy had not yet experienced any substantial passive selling, this tail risk has become a gray rhino closely watched by the market. The compression of mNAV means that the positive cycle of DAT has been interrupted: In a bull market, the stock price premium allows companies to raise funds through share issuance to increase their holdings of crypto assets at a cost lower than NAV, forming a positive cycle of issuance → buying coins → NAV increase → stock price increase → further issuance. However, when mNAV falls below 1.0, continued issuance will dilute the equity of existing shareholders, close the financing window, and may even trigger passive selling under debt repayment pressure. Grayscale also clearly pointed out in its outlook that DAT is unlikely to become a significant marginal variable in the market in 2026. Wall Street: Institutionalization process has not stopped, but demand side still needs to be repaired. Despite the weak market performance in the first quarter, the institutionalization process on Wall Street has not stopped. Morgan Stanley filed its first S-1 application for a Bitcoin ETF (ticker: MSBT) on January 6th, followed by a second amendment on March 20th. The NYSE subsequently issued an official listing notice on March 25th, a process significantly faster than market expectations. BlackRock's Bitcoin holdings account for approximately 60% of the market, with cumulative net inflows exceeding $63 billion, further solidifying its dominant position. Regarding XRP ETFs, seven spot products have been approved for listing between September and December 2025, with cumulative net inflows of approximately $1.44 billion. Institutions such as Goldman Sachs have already established positions through these products. On March 17th, the SEC and CFTC jointly designated XRP as a digital commodity, further strengthening the regulatory foundation. Furthermore, the IPO preparations of several crypto companies, including Anchorage Digital, OKX, Kraken, and Tether, are still underway, all listed as potential candidates for 2026. The significance of this institutionalization process lies in the fact that it has established an increasingly robust compliant financial infrastructure layer for the crypto market. From ETFs (equity arbitrage instruments) to IPOs (industry equity pricing anchors) to DAT (demand-taking assets), each channel is reducing the friction costs for traditional capital entering the crypto market. However, in the current macroeconomic environment, the supply-side development of these channels is far outpacing the demand-side willingness to invest. The release of institutional dividends requires a substantial improvement in risk appetite. Bitcoin's long-term scarcity narrative and institutional infrastructure remain unshaken, but in the short term, it has clearly been priced by the market as a high-beta macroeconomic trading tool, rather than a safe-haven asset independent of the traditional financial system. This cognitive restructuring will have a profound impact on asset allocation and risk management frameworks. AI Development: Large-Scale Model Iteration and the Progress of Crypto+Ai AI Large-Scale Model Iteration: Continued Rapid Development Over the past year, the iterative development of AI large-scale models can be described as rapid and ever-changing: More noteworthy is the cost curve: API call prices have decreased by over 80% in the past year, and AI's penetration into various industries has moved from the feasibility study stage to the economically driven stage. The economic barriers to large-scale deployment of downstream applications are disappearing, a speed that has exceeded the expectations of most industry participants. Crypto + AI: Narrative-Driven Approach Over the past year, the AI ​​industry has accelerated rapidly through leaps in model capabilities, an explosion of open-source frameworks, and a rapid decline in cost curves, with technology and commercialization advancing almost simultaneously. Faced with this wave, Crypto has also made numerous attempts to leverage its strengths to combine with AI in multiple directions: on the one hand, exploring application forms through narratives such as Agent platforms, DeFAI, and data layers; on the other hand, seeking verifiable demand and revenue loops through infrastructure such as decentralized computing networks and payment protocols. Arkstream Capital has identified nine main directions, and the following will outline the progress and realities of these attempts. Currently, within the entire Crypto+AI narrative, the segment with the most real revenue potential is the decentralized GPU computing network (DePIN × AI). Most other narratives remain largely narrative-driven and do not generate actual revenue. Project tokens are primarily driven by speculation, exhibiting a very pronounced hype cycle; once the hype cycle ends, the token price plummets. The decentralized GPU computing network (DePIN × AI) segment is more like a SaaS service, raising questions about the verifiability of its revenue data. Furthermore, major AI model companies have not adopted DePIN computing power on a large scale, and from a long-term commercial development perspective, it lacks the cost advantages of scalability compared to self-built computing power. The AI ​​industry has not paid for Crypto+AI, while the crypto industry tacitly accepts the fact that Crypto+AI is a product of short-term hype. Altcoin Market: Funding Differentiation and Continued Adjustment

Overall Market Performance

In the first quarter, the altcoin market continued the weak trend that began in the fourth quarter of last year. BTC Dominance traded in the 56%–59% range, and the CMC Altcoin Season Index remained below 35 for an extended period (above 75 indicates an altcoin season), indicating the market was consistently in a "Bitcoin Season" state. According to CoinGecko data, the total market capitalization of the global crypto market has decreased by approximately 16.8% compared to a year ago, and this decline was significantly amplified among tokens outside the Top 10. Many altcoins have fallen by 60%–80% from their cyclical highs, and 38% of altcoins have hit all-time lows. The Crypto Fear & Greed Index remained in the "extreme fear" zone for 46 consecutive days (as of the end of the quarter), hitting an all-time low of 5 on February 6. This is lower than the 6 during the Terra/Luna crash, the 8 during COVID, and the 10 during the FTX crash, marking the absolute lowest level since the index was created.

Crypto Fear & Greed Index Trend

https://alternative.me/crypto/fear-and-greed-index

Big Shanzhai / Small and Medium Shanzhai / BTC Market Value Comparison Chart -Tradingview

Thanks to ETFs, compared to the bull market peak in 2021, BTC's market capitalization reached a new high in 2025. However, the market capitalization of large altcoins (excluding stablecoins and BTC among the top 10 tokens by market capitalization) only slightly reached a new high, while the market capitalization of small and medium altcoins did not reach a new high. This is even more evident when looking at the market capitalization exchange rate of small and medium altcoins / BTC. The peak of the altcoin market coincided with the peak period of crypto innovation. Driven by DeFi and NFTs, the altcoin market was once very prosperous, but afterwards, the altcoin market entered a long bear market cycle with only periodic hot spots. During this process, the supply of altcoins increased, and the overall contraction of the market capitalization of small and medium altcoins, when converted into the market performance of individual altcoins, was even more disastrous. BTC's market capitalization reached a new high in 2025, mainly due to the approval of the BTC ETF and the injection of Wall Street funds, which brought it into the mainstream asset category. Large altcoins slightly benefited from the spillover of ETF funds, but ETFs did not have a significant positive impact on small and medium-sized altcoins. ArkStream Capital believes that the current predicament of the altcoin market is more structural than simply a cyclical adjustment. On the one hand, almost all new funding sources (ETFs, DAT) are locked in BTC and blue-chip assets, and small and medium-sized cryptocurrencies cannot provide innovative value to attract capital inflows. Small and medium-sized altcoins are becoming a gradually drying speculative market. "P small" will become a representative term in 2025; how to complete PvP in the secondary market during the period of market attention retention will become the main winning objective. The AI ​​Stock Market's Siphoning Effect on Crypto In the previous article, Arkstream Capital analyzed the continuous iteration of large-scale AI models and the attempts of Crypto+AI in nine directions. We found that the AI ​​industry has entered an economically driven phase. AI is already a fully-fledged industry with positive externalities. OpenAI currently has nearly 200 million daily active users. Total AI infrastructure spending is projected to reach $318 billion in 2025. Major companies have also launched an arms race, with the combined capital expenditures of Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, Microsoft, and Oracle increasing from $162.3 billion in 2022 to $448.3 billion in 2025. A large number of paying users and massive infrastructure investment have laid a solid foundation for the development of the AI ​​industry. However, Crypto+AI is still in the stage of narrative hype. From an investment return perspective, the returns of AI-related stocks in the stock market in 2025 far exceeded those of AI tokens in the crypto industry. Statistically, in 2025, AI-related tokens in the crypto market significantly underperformed the US stock market. The AI ​​stock market has created a siphon effect on Crypto. Major crypto CEXs such as Binance have also begun to list TradFi assets.

Binance Q1 Listings

After 2026, with the crypto market cooling down (Tokeninsight data shows that the total trading volume of the crypto market in Q1 2026 was $17.9 trillion, one-third less than Q4 2025, the lowest in recent quarters.), more and more crypto KOLs and retail investors are turning to the US stock market. The number of TradFi assets listed on Binance has already exceeded the number of crypto assets. It is expected that the trading volume of TradFi assets will grow significantly in Q2 2026.

... For players in the crypto industry, an increasingly real problem is that the appeal of native crypto assets, whether from a fundamental investment or speculative perspective, is declining compared to US stocks. This problem can only be solved with the emergence of the next wave of crypto innovation. Until then, cryptocurrencies must accept the reality of being siphoned off by the AI ​​industry. The Prisoner's Dilemma for Project Teams/Exchanges/Investors and the Application of Hedging Strategies Without external capital inflows, and with AI continuously diverting funds, every newly listed cryptocurrency presents a new prisoner's dilemma. Arkstream Capital compiled statistics on past VC investment projects listed on Binance (excluding projects with fair distribution like Meme). The price increase was compared between the price of TGE 1 or 2 years later and the closing price of TGE on that day. The data showed a widening trend of investment losses. The data for projects not listed on Binance was even more dismal. Currently, the industry commonly uses a 1-year Cliff clause for VC investments, resulting in a significant devaluation of tokens by the time VCs can claim them. Therefore, the reasonable application of hedging tools has become a strategy that VCs must consider. A crucial change is not mentioned in much public information. Compared to Web2 investments, the early advantage of the Crypto industry was the flexibility in asset release. Before 2021, a certain percentage of TGE was typically released, which greatly alleviated the investment pressure on VCs, allowing them to recoup some costs at the project's launch. However, after 2022, to comply with Binance's listing rules, most crypto projects adopted 1+3/1+4 unlocking clauses for private placements. Binance designed this to make the project's price chart look better after listing, reducing selling pressure. For project teams, they could allocate a certain amount of tokens to sell on the exchange under the guise of ecosystem/market capitalization management. For retail investors, who have always viewed VCs as counterparties, any restrictions on VCs would be welcomed. Nevertheless, in this ecosystem, for project teams, the optimal solution is to obtain some funds within the first month after launch by leveraging liquidity and hype, even though the price chart still shows a downward trend, especially during a bear market. This makes VCs perpetually disadvantaged in the entire Prisoner's Dilemma ecosystem, especially when crypto lacks innovation and is being siphoned off by the AI ​​industry. Primary market investment in crypto is extremely difficult. Therefore, Arkstream Capital essentially stopped primary market investment in 2025. A harsh reality is that for most projects, without prior hedging, it's practically equivalent to accepting investment losses. From an exit perspective, the gap between the paper returns of VC investments and the actual exit returns is widening. In an environment of continued liquidity contraction, the reliability of the traditional "investment—waiting for listing—secondary market exit" path is declining. ArkStream Capital observes that the difficulty of exiting pure primary market investments continues to rise, and the relative advantage of the pre-IPO OTC strategy is becoming increasingly apparent. Based on these observations, ArkStream Capital believes that in the current environment of liquidity contraction, unabated new token supply, and weakening market narrative momentum, the adjustment in the altcoin market is likely to continue. From a trading strategy perspective, shorting small-cap altcoins may be one of the few hedging methods with positive expected value. The upward trend of BTC Dominance indicates that the migration of funds from altcoins to Bitcoin is still underway, and the widespread post-listing price drops also provide ample targets for short sellers. Of course, shorting altcoins also faces tail risks. Any unexpected positive macroeconomic developments (such as accelerated interest rate cuts or geopolitical easing) or industry-level liquidity injections (such as the approval of new ETF categories) could trigger a sharp short squeeze. Therefore, strict position management and stop-loss discipline are essential. Overall, ArkStream Capital is cautious about the altcoin market's performance in the second quarter and recommends maintaining a defensive allocation in this sector. Summary: Pressures Far From Cleared. ArkStream Capital's baseline assessment for the second quarter and the second half of the year is conservative. The structural contradictions exposed in the first quarter—the sticky inflation resulting from tariffs and war, the policy mix of balance sheet reduction and high interest rates under Warsh's leadership, and the vicious cycle of oversupply and capital depletion in the altcoin market—none of which have the conditions for substantial reversal in the short term. The liquidity inflection point is likely to be further delayed. Under the dual inflationary pressures of tariffs and energy prices, the possibility of the CPI falling below 2.5% in the second quarter is extremely low, and zero interest rate cuts or even rate hikes throughout the year remain a scenario that needs serious consideration. Geopolitically, there is also a lack of basis for easing tensions. Even if the US-Iran conflict subsides in the short term, the political logic of a midterm election year dictates that the Trump administration will not abandon its aggressive stance. New geopolitical or trade shocks could emerge at any time, and the unpredictability and aggressiveness of policies before the November election are unlikely to substantially cool down. Systemic suppression of risk appetite will be the norm, not the exception. The clearing out of the altcoin market is also far from over. The peak of token unlocking will continue throughout the second and third quarters. In an environment lacking new funds, the selling pressure faced by small and medium-sized tokens will only intensify, not ease. Two marginal variables on which the market pins its hopes—the expansion of ETF categories and the productization of AI × Crypto—are unlikely to materialize in the short term: the timeline for approval of new products remains unclear, and AI Agent payment scenarios are still in the proof-of-concept stage; the probability of either contributing to substantial improvement is low. Based on the above assessment, ArkStream Capital will primarily adopt a defensive allocation strategy, controlling overall position size and leverage exposure, focusing on exits from existing investments and safe position management, and avoiding chasing any rebound signals that have not yet appeared. At this stage, protecting existing positions and conserving resources is a higher priority than speculating on a rebound.

data.title: What did the crypto community hear at Powell's last press conference?
data.description: On April 29th, interest rates remained unchanged. The Federal Reserve kept the federal funds rate between 3.50% and 3.75%. No rate cuts, no rate hikes—the outcome was entirely in line with expectations. But the market didn't hear "holding back." It heard: Inflation was still high, oil prices were still chaotic, the impact of tariffs hadn't been digested, internal disputes were brewing within the Fed, and Powell wasn't planning to leave immediately. This was Powell's last press conference as chairman, and the first time the market truly saw this mess before Kevin Warshoff took over. For the crypto world, the real issue isn't whether Powell leaves or not. It's whether money is still cheap. Interest rates haven't changed, but money has already flown out. Before the meeting, Bitcoin was hovering around $77,000. Not particularly strong, but not weak either, considering it was around $65,000 in early April. The market originally wanted to trade a familiar scenario: the Fed would pause first, then leave room for rate cuts, and stocks and crypto assets would continue to rise. The door wasn't closed, but a row of security guards stood at the entrance. Powell said that monetary policy has no predetermined path, and the next step depends on the data, outlook, and risk balance. In simpler terms: Don't rush to include interest rate cuts in price charts. Spot Bitcoin ETFs reacted first. According to SoSoValue data, on April 28th, US spot Bitcoin ETFs saw a net outflow of $89.6754 million. On April 29th (Eastern Time), the net outflow widened to approximately $138 million, marking the third consecutive day of outflows. This isn't an epic stampede, but the signal is clear: Institutions are unwilling to continue leveraging during this period of transition at the Federal Reserve, with inflation rebounding and oil prices surging. This type of capital rarely reverses course entirely based on a single statement, but it will slow down first. Especially when the macro environment hasn't provided a clear direction, buying less is itself a statement. Bitcoin is holding above $75,000, while Ethereum is testing the $2,300 level. Prices don't appear to have collapsed yet, but funds have already begun to withdraw. Four dissenting votes are uglier than not cutting rates. This meeting ostensibly had only one outcome: maintaining the interest rate unchanged. 8 votes in favor, 4 against. Stephen Miran wanted an immediate 25 basis point cut; Beth Hammack, Neel Kashkari, and Lorie Logan supported keeping rates unchanged but opposed retaining a dovish undertone in the statement. One person thought the cut was too slow, and three thought the wording was too soft. This isn't a matter of hawks and doves taking sides; rather, everyone agreed to hold off, but had already begun arguing about where to strike next. The Associated Press reported that this was the most dissenting votes since October 1992. In other words, Warsh hadn't even officially taken the chair yet, and he already saw a divided committee. For stocks and crypto assets, this is more troublesome than simply "not cutting interest rates." With interest rates currently stagnant, inflation rising, and officials disagreeing, every inflation and employment report could rewrite market expectations. What the market fears most isn't hawks or doves, but the uncertainty of who will suddenly change the script at the next meeting. The crypto world excels at grand narratives, but prices often only yield to liquidity. When the liquidity path becomes a fog, even the most beautiful narrative will be discounted. Powell's real dose of reality is inflation. The US unemployment rate in March was 4.3%, little changed; the Fed's preferred inflation gauge was 3.5% year-on-year, and core inflation was 3.2% year-on-year. Both inflation figures are still above the Fed's 2% target. On one hand, the Middle East conflict is pushing up global oil prices; on the other hand, tariffs continue to be passed on to commodity prices. Powell said that the Fed has always assumed that tariffs would cause a one-off price increase that would then disappear over time, but the next two quarters must see it actually happen. His meaning is quite straightforward: Theoretically, the central bank can penetrate short-term oil price shocks; in reality, the oil price shock hasn't subsided, and inflation has been above target for several years, so now is not the time to blindly cut interest rates. This statement, when applied to the cryptocurrency market, takes a different form: Don't mistake "pausing interest rate hikes" for "immediate monetary easing." In the past few years, whenever the macroeconomy faltered, the market would fantasize about interest rate cuts; whenever stocks and cryptocurrencies fell, everyone would imagine the Federal Reserve intervening to rescue the market. But this time, inflation stems from oil prices and tariffs; interest rate cuts might rescue asset prices, but they might also reignite inflation expectations. So it can only wait. Waiting is a punishment for a highly leveraged market. As long as real interest rates don't come down, the cost of holding positions remains. For the crypto market, this is more painful than a simple "no interest rate cut for now." Because it's not a short-term pain, but rather a continuation of the tightening of the very pipe the bull market desperately needs. Many people interpret Kevin Warsh's potential succession as a new positive for the crypto market. There are reasons for this. He is considered to have a better understanding of market signals and has also viewed Bitcoin as an important asset and a gauge of policy pressure. His opposition to the Federal Reserve issuing digital dollars directly to ordinary people at his Senate hearing is not bad news for private stablecoins. However, if you think Warsh will immediately celebrate in the crypto world, you're mistaken. If Warsh ultimately takes over, he won't be inheriting a new machine, but a smoking dashboard: inflation remains high, oil prices are volatile, tariffs haven't been fully absorbed, and four dissenting votes are on the table. More importantly, Powell hasn't truly left. He explicitly stated that he will continue to serve as a governor for a period after his chairmanship ends on May 15th. The Associated Press report noted that this would be the first time since 1948 that a former Federal Reserve chairman has remained on the board of governors. This has two sides to Bitcoin. On one hand, political pressure eroding central bank independence could lead some to rediscover the significance of "non-sovereign assets." On the other hand, narratives cannot cover financing costs. If interest rates remain high and inflation remains sluggish, the market may be trading not a "knowledgeable chairman," but a "more unpredictable Federal Reserve." In other words, Warsh may bring a more favorable long-term outlook to the crypto world, but short-term pricing power still rests with inflation and interest rates. This is very much in line with the crypto world. The good news is real, and the bad news is real too. The long-term door hasn't been closed. The Digital Asset Market Clarity Act has passed the House of Representatives and is now officially in the Senate Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs Committee. It attempts to redefine the regulation of crypto assets in the United States: giving the Commodity Futures Trading Commission a more central role in crypto asset trading while retaining the Securities and Exchange Commission's authority in some issuance and trading stages. Stablecoins have also been placed in more formal policy discussions. A White House Council of Economic Advisers report on April 8 stated that, under conventional assumptions, banning stablecoin yields would only lead to banks issuing an additional $2.1 billion in loans, roughly 0.02% of total loans, but users would receive approximately $800 million less in benefits. Even using the most aggressive assumptions, expanding the stablecoin market to about six times its current size, the additional loans would only amount to $531 billion, corresponding to a 4.4% increase in bank loans. These are all long-term positives. However, in the short term, all stories must first pass the Federal Reserve's hurdle. Powell's last appearance on the podium did not leave the market with a pleasing conclusion. He left behind a more practical question: When the crypto market is finally about to be bailed out by regulations, can it weather a period of even higher monetary costs? The money hasn't gone far, it's just become more expensive.

The NDRC's decision is a clear statement on this model. The timing of Manus's explosive popularity coincides with the point at which the AI ​​field began to gain true global market recognition. The emergence of DeepSeek has prompted a re-evaluation of the engineering expertise of domestic AI teams, with Manus following closely behind, further validating the capabilities of domestic teams in Agent development. The actual capabilities in this area. The formation of these capabilities relies on the long-term support of China's data environment, engineering talent, and entrepreneurial ecosystem. Selling it entirely to an overseas technology company involves more than just commercial mergers and acquisitions from a regulatory perspective. AI, as a core technology area, is receiving increasing attention from cross-border investment reviews, a trend that is accelerating globally. On April 24th, just days before the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) made its decision, some leading domestic AI companies also received updates on relevant compliance notices, requiring them to complete approval procedures for accepting external capital. These signals appearing in clusters indicate that the scope has expanded beyond a single case. However, in Manus's case, some employees have already integrated into the internal team, funds have been transferred, and technical integration has reportedly begun. Manus has not issued any statement. This is a clear signal to participants in the AI ​​industry.
