Where technology comes from determines where it can go; cross-border mergers and acquisitions are shifting from a path of technology diffusion to a new consideration for technology flow.

data.title: Whose Dollars? The USDT in Hormuz and the Disorderly Dollar System
data.description:
One night in March 2026, a fully loaded oil tanker slowed down at the narrowest point of the Strait of Hormuz. A Persian command came over the radio: Stop, pay the fee, or there will be no escort. The fee was $1 per barrel, $2 million for a fully loaded VLCC. USDT, Bitcoin, and RMB were accepted, but USD was not. The captain checked his phone and saw a USDT wallet address sent by a middleman. He hesitated for a few seconds—not because of the amount, but because he suddenly realized that this money would be transferred in a way unseen by the world's largest economy. No SWIFT messages, no intermediary banks, no compliance checks. On the same day, 6,000 kilometers away in Lagos, a grocery store owner named Emeka checked his phone to see the monthly remittance his brother had sent from London. For the past six years, his brother had been sending money every month. Emeka received the dollars, exchanged them for naira on the black market, and the difference between the dollar and the official exchange rate was his profit margin for keeping his shop running. Today is May 1st. New regulations from the Central Bank of Nigeria have come into effect—all remittances from overseas are no longer accepted in dollars and must be settled in naira. Emeka's screen no longer displays dollars, but a string of numbers converted at the official exchange rate. The black market rate is 40% higher than the official rate. The money his brother sends hasn't decreased, but what he receives has shrunk by a third. One entity uses USDT to settle tolls in the Strait of Hormuz, bypassing the eighty-year-old dollar clearing system. Another entity, in Africa's largest economy, shut down a decades-old dollar remittance channel with a single executive order. Both point to the same fact: the dollar is still the same dollar. But the system built around it—clearing, licensing, regulation—is falling apart. I. The Dollar Empire Built on Shipping Routes In 1987, at the height of the Iran-Iraq War, oil tankers in the Persian Gulf became easy targets. Shipping insurance prices soared, and shipowners dared not sail. Kuwait came up with a solution—to seek help from the United States. The US condition was simple: fly your oil tankers under the American flag. Kuwait complied. Eleven oil tankers were re-registered as US vessels and escorted by US troops through the Strait of Hormuz. This operation was codenamed "Operation Earnest Will," but its true significance went far beyond escort—it declared to the world one thing: the US controlled this shipping route. Forty years later, in 2026, Pakistan was doing the same thing. The Pakistani Ministry of Foreign Affairs extended an invitation to global commodity traders: were there any ships willing to temporarily fly the Pakistani flag? The flag was hoisted, and the Iranian Navy provided escort. The price of a flag in 2026: $2 million. The escort changed from the US Navy to the Iranian Navy, and the stars and stripes on the flag were replaced with a crescent moon. The script remained unchanged, but the cast was completely different. This is not a coincidence, but a structure that has been operating for hundreds of years: Whoever controls the straits controls trade; whoever controls the physical channels of trade has the right to decide what currency is used for settlement. The lifeline of global trade is not in the trading floors of Wall Street, but hangs on a few narrow waterways. The Strait of Hormuz carries 20% of the world's oil and gas traffic, while the Strait of Malacca carries 25% of maritime trade. The United States doesn't need to control the entire Pacific—it only needs to control these key chokepoints. The 2026 US National Defense Strategy summarized this logic in four words: "peace through strength." The attack on Iran wasn't for Iran's sake. The Strait of Hormuz is also a crucial route for 40% of China's crude oil imports and a key node in Russia's "North-South Corridor." One military action simultaneously cuts off the chokepoints of two alternative pipelines. But physical control is merely the foundation. On top of this foundation lies a sophisticated layer of financial control. SWIFT makes every cross-border transaction visible to the United States. The OFAC sanctions list allows any asset to be frozen. Correspondent banking networks ensure every hop undergoes compliance review. In early 2026, former IMF Chief Economist Kenneth Rogoff made a statement at a Harvard lecture that silenced the audience for a few seconds: "Everyone is watching the front end—what currency is oil priced in, what channels are used for settlement. But what truly determines victory isn't the front end, but the back end. Who sees the transaction? Where does the information go?" In the dollar system, there's only one answer to these two questions: the US sees it, and the information remains within the US system. The core of control isn't the pipeline itself, but who is watching what flows through it. The physical layer controls the flow of goods. The financial back end controls the flow of information. Together, these two layers form a complete operating system. In peacetime, you only see the financial layer—SWIFT, credit cards, bank transfers. The physical layer is invisible. In the 300 milliseconds it takes you to swipe your card to buy coffee, you don't feel a navy guarding your transaction. Peace is the rising tide. Dollar liquidity has submerged everything; the surface is flat, and the underwater structure is invisible. Until someone sets up cannons at the choke point. The tide begins to recede. The logic of the dollar empire is top-down control, but the logic within the cracks is bottom-up growth, a wild, untamed growth.

II. Six Things That Happen in 300 Milliseconds When You Swipe Your Card

There are only three ways for an empire to maintain the dollar order: war, sanctions, and rewriting the rules. In the spring of 2026, all three methods were present simultaneously—and each one was dismantling the functions that were bundled together in the dollar system.

To understand this "dismantling," you must first understand what the "bundling" is all about.

You swipe your card to buy a cup of coffee. In 300 milliseconds, you think one thing has happened—"payment." In reality, at least six things are completed simultaneously.

A price is anchored (pricing), a value is transferred (settlement), a reserve is drawn (reserve), a legal commitment is executed (legal protection), a compliance screening is passed (political approval), and a compliance screening is completed. A physical pipeline was used (shipping channel). Six things, one currency, one sovereignty. You don't feel their presence, just as you don't feel the six components of air. Until the spring of 2026, the tide recedes. 2.1 The War in the Strait of Hormuz: When a line of small print on the insurance policy disappears. March 5, Strait of Hormuz. P&I war insurance was withdrawn. What was severed that day wasn't just a financial pipeline. What was severed was something more fundamental than finance—the physical channel itself. Without insurance, ship owners dared not sail. Without shipping, there is no trade. Without trade, the word "payment" loses its premise. Everyone is discussing SWIFT and sanctions, but in Hormuz, what was severed first wasn't information, but a small line of text on the insurance policy. Without insurance, the flag appeared, and USDT entered the mainstream. The IRGC's use of USDT for fees is actually quite precise: it borrows the dollar's pricing mechanism—the world still measures the value of a barrel of oil in dollars—but completely bypasses the US's right to know and freeze the transaction. The pricing is still in dollars, but the dollar's authorization no longer goes through the US. When Luke Gromen was interviewed in late March and asked about his views on the situation, he only said one sentence: "Whether Hormuz is open is the only important thing. Everything else is noise." Arthur Hayes went even further in his article "No Trade Zone," published around the same time. He described a financial chain: countries sell US Treasury bonds → buy gold → exchange for RMB or crypto → pay tolls. On one chain, pricing, settlement, reserves, and political authorization—several functions originally bundled in the dollar—are simultaneously dismantled, each finding different carriers. The practice of levying taxes across the Taiwan Strait has been around for six hundred years. What has changed is not the taxation itself, but the settlement layer. 2.2 Russian Sanctions: The Dollar Hasn't Moved, But the Value Transfer Has Been Completed. Four years ago, Russia was kicked out of SWIFT, and the mainstream assessment was that it would be "paralyzed." The reality four years later: 99% of Russia-China trade is settled in rubles and yuan, and the $228 billion in bilateral trade has almost completely bypassed the dollar. Outside of the fiat currency pipeline, a ruble-based stablecoin, A7A5, issued in Kyrgyzstan, has quietly processed over $100 billion in transactions. The Russian central bank is preparing to enshrine the grey crypto pipeline into law on July 1st. There was no paralysis. Three new pipelines grew. But even new pipelines present new problems. India purchased large quantities of Russian crude oil at a discount, settling in rupees. It seemed the problem was solved—the pipelines bypassed the dollar, and trade continued. But Russia soon encountered a predicament: billions of rupees that it couldn't spend. Technically, the rupee could be used for cross-border payments—settlement was possible. But the rupee is a semi-convertible currency, restricted under the capital account—it couldn't buy what it wanted, nor could it be transferred to where it wanted. A currency that could be used for payments couldn't be used to store value. The two functions of a currency were split. The final solution didn't come from the payments industry, but from the capital markets. In late 2025, Sberbank launched a fund linked to the Indian Nifty 50 index, allowing trade surpluses to be "digested" through the Indian stock market. In March 2026, Indian refiners went a step further, settling Russian oil transactions directly in yuan and dirhams. Zoltan Pozsar marked the beginning of this turning point when the Russia-Ukraine war broke out in 2022. He said that the day the G7 froze Russia's central bank reserves, US Treasury bonds transformed from "risk-free assets" into "politically risky assets." He called this the end of Bretton Woods II—the monetary order backed by US Treasury bonds was being replaced by an order backed by gold and commodities. Four years later, in the Russia-India corridor, this replacement is underway. The dollar didn't move, but the value transfer was complete. The water didn't go through the original pipeline, but it flowed to where it was meant to be. 2.3 The Reshaping of Lagos: Pipeline Replacement Now back to Lagos. Back to the grocery store in Emeka. Emeka's predicament looks completely different from the tankers in Hormuz and the rupees in Moscow. No war, no sanctions, no naval blockade. The Central Bank of Nigeria did it itself. In 2020, CBN ordered: overseas remittances must be paid in US dollars. The logic then was to leverage the credit of the US dollar to attract foreign exchange inflows. On May 1, 2026, CBN ordered that overseas remittances must be settled in Naira. The logic flipped 180 degrees—an attempt to retain foreign exchange and control the exchange rate. The same institution, the same tools, completely reversed direction in six years. CBN dismantled the pipeline, but repairs immediately began on the streets. P2P USDT trading volume rose in the week the new regulations took effect. Informal currency exchange dealers were busier. Sovereignty was stripped of a layer, and the people patched the cracks. You blocked the official channels, and water seeped from the cracks in the bricks on the street. Nigeria didn't expose American sovereignty. It exposed CBN's own sovereignty. It tells the public a fact that most people have never realized: you think you are freely receiving dollars, but in fact you have been receiving dollars within my permitted scope all along. Now I am withdrawing that permission. Sanctions are someone else dismantling your pipeline. Policy is dismantling your own pipeline. The effect is the same. 2.4 Dubai's Exit: Twenty Years of Shadow Banking, Exit in Three Weeks Finally, it's Dubai. On April 28, the UAE announced its withdrawal from OPEC. Effective May 1—the same day as Nigeria's new regulations. What the UAE did was not just withdraw from an organization, but simultaneously reclaim three things. Pricing Power: Shedding the 3.2 million barrels per day production quota, releasing 4.8 million barrels per day of production capacity. Export Route: Fujairah Port, located on the Gulf of Oman side, physically bypasses Hormuz, and 1.7 million barrels per day have already been shipped from here. Clearing Control: In March 2026, UAE security forces arrested dozens of currency exchange brokers in Dubai—these individuals had been operating unlicensed exchange points in the narrow alleys of the Dejairah district for years, with an office, two mobile phones, and ledgers in their heads. They were the capillaries of the gray finance between Dubai and Tehran. Trade between Dubai and Iran before the war surged from $4 billion to $12 billion between 2005 and 2009. It was a period of tacit ambiguity—neither side questioned the other's financial situation. Dubai spent twenty years acting as Iran's shadow bank. It withdrew from that role in three weeks. Four locations, four different ways of dismantling. In peacetime, these functions operated seamlessly within the dollar system, creating a unified "global payment system." In the spring of 2026, as the tide receded, this system was torn apart. Each function, once exposed, begins to independently seek its own vehicle—the RMB, the Dirham, USDT, gold, Nifty 50 fund shares, or even a Pakistani flag. The payment channels haven't been severed, but the dollar system is disintegrating. The components are still functioning—just no longer driven by a single sovereign power. This isn't de-dollarization. No single currency can simultaneously replace all six functions of the dollar. The dollar remains the pricing anchor for global trade—even the IRGC uses USDT, pegged to the dollar, for its fees. But the dollar's jurisdiction is fragmenting—more and more transactions are occurring behind the scenes, unseen by the US. The dollar is still the dollar; what's disordered is the system built around it. III. Imperial Guns and the Patience of Flowing Water Every month, a batch of gold bars departs from London or Switzerland, destined for Beijing. The People's Bank of China has been increasing its gold reserves for several consecutive months without interruption. It has never explained why in any press conference. But the whole world understands this action. Looking back at the four cases, every crack was created by the empire's own guns. Sanctions against Russia—4 years later, 99% of Sino-Russian trade is de-dollarized. Blockade of the Hormuz—Iran uses USDT and the RMB to open up alternative settlements. Freezing central bank reserves—central banks around the world are increasingly repatriating their gold reserves. Every time the dollar is used as a weapon, it tells the world the same thing: your dollar assets are not your property, but a license from the United States. This license can be revoked at any time. Ray Dalio calls this "capital war"—not the Cold War, not the trade war, but the weaponization of capital itself. Financial historian Adam Tooze touches on the same contradiction from another perspective: the dollar system has functioned for eighty years because the United States is seen as predictable and rules-based. Every successful sanctions erode this premise—the rules change from "applicable to everyone" to "it depends on who you are." Maintenance and disintegration happen simultaneously. The more an empire uses the dollar system to punish its adversaries, the more it demonstrates to the world that this system is not a neutral infrastructure—but a weapon that can be withdrawn at any time. The dollar hasn't changed, but people's trust in the dollar system has. And once it happens, there's no going back. Back in Emeka's grocery store. His brother will be sending money from London again next month. But Emeka had already found a USDT exchange on the street corner—the rate was 40% better than CBN's official channel, and the arrival time was reduced from three days to three minutes. Even if CBN withdraws this policy tomorrow, Emeka won't go back. Because he has already discovered the real costs associated with the old pipeline, and the entrance to the new pipeline is right on the street corner. Regardless of the outcome of the Hormuz conflict—whether Iran wins, the US wins, or the negotiations in Islamabad succeed—the P2P USDT network will not disappear because of the ceasefire agreement. 99% of ruble-yuan settlements will not switch back to the dollar because of the lifting of sanctions. The Fujairah bypass will not be abandoned because the Strait of Hormuz reopens. The IRGC financial network severed by Dubai will not be rebuilt through diplomacy. The louder the empire's guns roar, the deeper the water seeps. The deeper the water seeps, the more the guns must not stop. This cycle has already begun. The water found a new path, but it won't flow back on its own. IV. Shadow Dollars Within these cracks, something unprecedented has grown. The IRGC prefers USDT over Bitcoin when charging in the Hormuz region for a simple reason: Bitcoin lacks a price anchor, is too volatile, and unsuitable for trade settlement. USDT parasitizes the pricing credibility of the dollar, but grows within the cracks of dollar jurisdiction. What IRGC needs is not "de-dollarization," but "using dollars but without going through the US." USDT precisely meets this need. Two types of dollar-denominated stablecoins, two distances from US sovereignty. Circle's USDC cooperates with OFAC to freeze blacklisted addresses; Tether's USDT often does not cooperate. In the sanctions corridor—Iran, Russia, Venezuela—USDT on Tron holds absolute dominance because of the lowest compliance friction, fastest confirmation, and lowest cost. The market's vote with its feet shows that the farther away from US sovereignty, the more people use it. The evolution of Russia provides a complete timeline. A7A5 stablecoins handled over $100 billion in traffic. The EU took down Garantex. One down, another grows. Another down, another grows. The week Garantex was taken down, its successor, Grinex, launched. The day Grinex was taken down, the next name was already registered. By April 2026, the EU's 20th round of sanctions no longer targeted individual platforms—it imposed an industry-wide ban on the entire Russian crypto industry, taking effect on May 24. Crypto assets that have been exposed to Russian links may be labeled "dirty coins," like those from Iran and North Korea. You build a dam, and water will seep out from the bottom. If you reinforce the base of the dam, the water will bypass it from both sides. Even if you block both sides, the water will still seep into the soil downstream. Sanctions cannot eliminate demand; they only make the ways to meet demand more dispersed, more covert, and more difficult to trace. USDT is neither a substitute for the dollar nor an extension of the dollar. It is an unauthorized fork of the dollar-based sovereign system—retaining the core but stripping away the licensing agreement. There is a deeper paradox here: disintegration and reinforcement occur simultaneously. USDT reinforces the pricing inertia of the US dollar—global sanctions corridors remain anchored to the dollar, with even the IRGC not choosing to price in RMB. However, USDT is simultaneously undermining the dollar's jurisdiction—more and more transactions are occurring behind the scenes, unseen by the US. The targets of sanctions are transacted using the shadow currency of the sanctioned. The more effective the sanctions, the stronger this shadow becomes. Sanctions do not eliminate the demand for the dollar, only the permission to use it. The moment demand and permission are separated, the structural position of the shadow dollar is established. Sanctions strengthen demand, demand strengthens USDT, and USDT strengthens the pricing inertia of the dollar. A self-feeding cycle. V. Conclusion The oil tanker eventually passed through the strait. The captain paid $2 million in USDT. No American bank saw the transaction. But the crude oil on board was still priced in US dollars after arriving at its destination port. Buyers quoted prices in US dollars, sellers calculated profits in US dollars, and insurance companies estimated losses in US dollars. The pricing was still in US dollars. Only its permit no longer passed through the United States. In the spring of 2026, the tide receded. What was revealed wasn't anything new—it was something that had always been underwater. Whose order, whose permission, whose dollars?

data.title: What homework is still unfinished after the Manus acquisition was banned?
data.description: The news of Meta's acquisition of Manus sparked a cacophony of opinions. Some cheered, some frowned, some worried about the enthusiasm of foreign venture capital firms, and some worried about the potential harm to Chinese innovation. Let me state my opinion first: it should be stopped, and more importantly, it cannot be merely stopped. Banning the transaction is just one action; what's truly important is the series of lessons that need to be learned. This article wants to discuss four things with you: 1. Manus is far from being the first, but hopefully he will be the last; 2. Who pushed the Manus onto this path; 3. Besides calling a halt, what else should we do? 4. If you happen to be an entrepreneur hesitating between going left or right... We can talk about what's on our minds. The unspoken understanding among the four companies. If you only look at Manus, you might think this is making a mountain out of a molehill. However, if we look at the timeline a bit further, we will find that this time the regulatory action was not aimed at any specific individual, but rather at many people who are no longer present. Zoom: The First Memorable Model Zoom's founder, Eric Yuan, went to the United States from China in 1997. Many people think Zoom is a purely American company, but for a long time, its core R&D team was based in China—with large engineering teams in Suzhou, Hefei, and Hangzhou. Before the COVID-19 pandemic, over 70% of Zoom's R&D staff were in China. Chinese engineers were cheaper, delivered faster, and produced consistent quality. Zoom built its global market presence using this "US front-end + Chinese back-end" structure. During the COVID-19 pandemic, Zoom became increasingly popular due to the trend of working from home. However, due to some flaws in its data management, politicians like Pelosi began to hype it up, questioning its security and even its loyalty, and demanding that Eric Yuan attend a congressional hearing, questioning his alleged improper ties with China. This was followed by systemic pressure on Zoom to "de-Sinicize"—moving R&D out of China, reducing the proportion of Chinese employees, and adjusting data storage locations. This process did not trigger a strong reaction in China at the time, but its "decisive" separation and seemingly smooth business migration provided a reference and model for some forces to later promote "de-Sinicization". Heygen: A Template for Going More Thoroughly Heygen's story is more typical. Its predecessor was Shiyun Technology, founded in Shenzhen in 2020 by Xu Zhuo, a former Chinese engineer at Snapchat. The company focuses on AI-powered digital human and video generation. Its early core team of 30 people was almost entirely Chinese. Early funding came from Chinese venture capital firms such as Baidu Ventures, Sequoia China, ZhenFund, and IDG Capital, making it a purely Chinese startup. The company became known to netizens for its product, which allowed Taylor Swift to speak Chinese in a video. A turning point occurred around 2023. According to media reports, Heygen received $5.6 million in funding led by US venture capital firm Conviction Partners, bringing its overall valuation to $75 million. Sarah Guo, founder of Conviction Partners, directly replaced Sequoia China on HeyGen's board of directors and publicly stated, "The environment has changed significantly over the past year and a half. Xu Zhuo made it very clear that we need to understand our investor base, our user base, and our data center, and we must also understand that we cannot be influenced by the government." Following this, Heygen secured $60 million in funding led by US VC Benchmark, reaching a valuation of approximately $500 million. This allowed them to completely remove the aforementioned Chinese investors from the company, and the brand name was changed from "Shiyun" to "Heygen". Heygen has cancelled its Chinese company. Employees have two choices: relocate to North America or resign. Now, on Heygen's official website, the introductions of several Chinese faces show no trace of their past work or life in China. Manus: Similar Path, But Bigger Impact. At Manus, the script is almost identical. Xiao Hong comes from a small city in Jiangxi Province and studied software engineering at a university in Wuhan. In 2022, he founded "The Butterfly Effect." Wuhan provided him with high-quality human resources and office subsidies, and domestic self-media used sensational headlines to make Manus famous. However, according to descriptions given to foreign media by former employees, Manus was structured in the Cayman Islands from the beginning, with an entity in Singapore, indicating that going global was a "pre-planned path" rather than a "later choice." The real tipping point, similarly, occurred after Benchmark's investment. A former employee revealed that shortly after Benchmark's entry, Manus notified employees to completely shut down its China operations, and employees unwilling to relocate were laid off. The rest is history. Miromind: A More Complex Sequel Before the Manus incident had fully subsided, Miromind entered the public eye. Miromind is an AI company founded by Chen Tianqiao, the founder of Shanda Group, registered in Singapore, and publicly claims to be headquartered in California. In May 2025, it hired Dai Jifeng, an associate professor at Tsinghua University, as its chief scientist. Dai Jifeng previously served as executive research director at SenseTime and is a well-known scholar in the field of computer vision. According to a report in The Washington Post, Miromind completed most of its early research in China. In January 2026, the company required its Chinese employees to relocate to Singapore or Japan, and even launched a "relocation incentive policy"—offering salary increases, stock options, and subsidies to those willing to relocate—but this policy was quickly withdrawn. Dai Jifeng subsequently resigned, stating to foreign media that the reason for his departure was "inability to accept the company's requirement for AI researchers to relocate out of China." Miromind then issued an internal notice alleging that Jifeng had "breached commercial integrity," stating that he had proactively resigned in January of this year, demanding to take 15% of the company's shares and core domestic personnel with him, and even demanding "unconditional, indefinite, irrevocable, and free" use of Miromind's intellectual property. This matter remains unresolved, but regardless of the specific circumstances, they all point to the same fact: a mature set of operational methods has emerged regarding the cross-border flow of AI talent and technology from China, backed by well-established resources, funding, and established pathways. span > leaf="">Overall migration", Manus's"More than Ocean realization", Miromind's"Attempted relocation". Four stories, four attitudes, but everyone basically received a similar script. This is no coincidence. Who is pushing these companies to "de-Sinicize"? The main force driving this is a group of US dollar venture capital (VCs). To understand why they are pushing this forward, we must first understand their "bread and butter"—LPs (limited partners). Where does the bulk of the money for these US dollar VCs come from? American university endowments, pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, and family offices. These limited partners (LPs) have also had a tough time in recent years: on the one hand, the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States (CFIUS) has been scrutinizing "China exposure" more and more strictly; on the other hand, executive orders restricting investment in China, issued during the Biden era and continued during the Trump era, directly included Chinese investments in AI, semiconductors, and quantum computing within the scope of prohibitions or reporting. As a result, LPs' attitudes have changed. Many funds have found that when raising new rounds of funding, they cannot secure funding if their portfolios contain significant "Chinese AI assets." Some LPs have even explicitly included "China exposure" clauses in their investment agreements, requiring funds to proactively avoid it. This pressure is transmitted layer by layer from LP to GP to investee company. Along this chain, the names of several US dollar VCs appear repeatedly. A typical example is Benchmark—a key player in the Manus saga. Former employees revealed to foreign media that it was only after Benchmark's entry that Manus completely shut down its China operations and laid off employees who hadn't relocated. This long-established Silicon Valley fund has always prided itself on "not investing in China." Its decision to invest in Manus was contingent on Manus becoming a "non-Chinese company." Furthermore, according to former employees, US regulators are still investigating whether Benchmark's investment in Manus violated restrictions on AI investments in China. Conviction Partners, which was very active in Heygen's relocation, is another typical example. Its founder, Sarah Guo (former partner at Greylock), looks at projects at a relatively early stage. According to disclosures from the legal and venture capital communities, US dollar funds (especially those involved in Reverse CFIUS-sensitive areas) typically employ the following "mandatory or semi-mandatory" measures when investing in Chinese-background companies: First, Trigger Rights: A mandatory repurchase clause is included in the investment agreement (TS). If the company faces Reverse CFIUS scrutiny or is designated a "Restricted Foreign Entity" by the US Treasury Department, the US dollar fund has the right to require the company or founders to repurchase its shares at a high price, allowing for a safe exit. Second, Physical and Personnel Relocation: Core founders, CEO, and CTO are required to physically relocate overseas (e.g., to Singapore, the US, or Canada). If there is a domestic R&D team, it must be proven that it is merely an "outsourced cost center" and has no access to core models or US user data; in extreme cases (such as Manus), the domestic team may be directly laid off. Third, Cutting Off the Domestic Market: The product is explicitly required not to provide services to the Chinese mainland market (e.g., blocking Chinese IP addresses, not providing a Chinese interface, not supporting domestic payment tools) to prove that its business is unrelated to China. How to deal with the "de-Sinicization" countercurrent? This regulatory halt to the Manus acquisition sends a signal to the market, but the signal itself cannot solve the problem. After Manus, there will certainly be other companies, other paths, and other variations. Because behind the huge bubble of AI projects, behind every "relocation" lies a profit with a return of tens of times. Following the time-tested wisdom of "prevention is worse than cure," the first priority should be to ensure that startup teams feel secure in operating in China, preventing them from being misled by lofty "globalization" narratives (which are often essentially "Americanization" narratives). Why do entrepreneurs readily follow the lead of US dollar VCs? A large part of the reason is that the domestic entrepreneurial environment still has some real pain points that, if left unresolved, will lead entrepreneurs to "vote with their feet." On the business side, which is of utmost importance to entrepreneurs, Chinese companies are unable to legally, on a large scale, and at low cost obtain NVIDIA's latest generation of high-end AI chips due to the U.S. Department of Commerce's export controls (Entity List and chip ban). This results in severe computing power bottlenecks for model training and high-concurrency inference. Furthermore, AI agent products like Manus rely heavily on accessing the world's top closed-source models (such as Anthropic's Claude series and OpenAI's GPT series). However, both OpenAI and Anthropic have explicitly implemented strict API bans on mainland Chinese IP addresses and entities registered in China. If these startups retain their Chinese corporate entities or Chinese servers, they will directly lose the right to use the world's most powerful "AI brain." Even if they use some methods to circumvent this blockade, these methods cannot be embedded in a qualified business model when facing due diligence from investors. On the market side, the European and American markets have a very mature software payment culture. Whether for enterprises (To B) or individuals (To C), users are accustomed to paying a subscription fee ranging from $20 to $200 per month. As the founder of Manus stated, overseas users' willingness to pay is likely several times higher than that in China. Coupled with the leverage of exchange rates, this represents a market with profits dozens of times greater than the domestic market. Besides profits, the "extreme involution" and customization quagmire of the domestic market also put pressure on many startups. Competition in the domestic software and AI market is exceptionally fierce. Consumers (To C) are accustomed to the "free + advertising" model and have a low willingness to pay directly for pure software tools; while business (To B) clients face strong demands from enterprise customers for "private deployment" and "heavy customization," which can severely strain the R&D efforts of a startup with only a few dozen employees, leading to a quagmire of project outsourcing and hindering product standardization and scalability. Finally, regarding financing and monetization, the current domestic listing and exit channels are indeed not smooth enough—A-shares are not friendly to VIE structures, Hong Kong stocks have fluctuating liquidity, the Science and Technology Innovation Board has strict review requirements for pure AI application companies, and the Beijing Stock Exchange is still in its incubation period… This forces entrepreneurs to either queue for five years to list on the A-share market or go overseas with the US dollar. Even if financing is successful, there is still much room for improvement in the convenience of equity incentives and option exercise. These topics deserve a separate paper; their core message is to transform entrepreneurs from "unable to leave" to "unwilling to leave." Don't mistake "globalization" for narrow "Americanization." Of course, I also want to offer some honest advice to entrepreneurs, without resorting to empty rhetoric. You may have underestimated the true potential of China's AI market. Many entrepreneurs, swayed by dollar-denominated venture capital, believe that "the Chinese market is failing and we should go to the US." This judgment is inaccurate. Let's look at the data first. China has 1.4 billion people, over 1 billion internet users, and the world's most comprehensive range of industries—what does this mean? It means that China is a unique global supermarket capable of providing sufficient training materials and application scenarios for AI. For a medical AI product, the US might have samples from 50 hospitals, while China might have samples from 500. A single intelligent customer service product can only cover a limited number of customers for a US SaaS company, while in China, the e-commerce, finance, and local services sectors alone are enough to support dozens of unicorns. Furthermore, regarding engineers, the total number of STEM graduates trained annually in China exceeds the combined total of the US, Europe, and Japan. In terms of engineering delivery efficiency per unit of computing power, Chinese teams still lead globally. This is not an exaggeration; it's a real cost and speed advantage. DeepSeek, Tongyi, Doubao, and Kimi were able to catch up in large-scale model capabilities in a short time thanks to this foundation. Finally, regarding applications... While the US leads in basic AI models, China's vitality in application implementation is no less impressive. The WeChat ecosystem, Alipay mini-program ecosystem, and Douyin ecosystem—these "super apps"—are brimming with opportunities for AI application. In the B2B sector, the potential for AI transformation in China's manufacturing, logistics, agriculture, and healthcare industries is several times that of the US market. Finally, let's talk about policy. In the past two years, the support for the AI ​​industry from the central to local levels has been unprecedented. Computing power subsidies, data openness, scenario openness, financing connections—by fully utilizing these advantages, a company can achieve a valuation of hundreds of millions of US dollars, have real cash flow, and real barriers to entry domestically. Why exchange them for a "ticket" to the US that could be torn up at any time? I want to specifically discuss the word "globalization." In recent years, a particular rhetoric has become very popular—"If you don't go overseas, you won't have a global perspective." "If you don't go to the US, you won't grow big." But if you think about it carefully, the "globalization" in that statement actually only points to one place: Europe and America, especially the United States. This is a kind of instilled mindset. The real map of globalization is much larger than that. Look at the "Belt and Road" initiative, which includes more than 60 countries, more than 4 billion people, and a GDP exceeding one-third of the global total. Central Asia, Eastern Europe, the Middle East, Southeast Asia, and South Asia—these regions have a much higher acceptance of Chinese products, technology, and business models than Europe and the United States. Looking at the Global South, over 50 countries in Africa, over 30 countries in Latin America, and the 10 ASEAN countries together form a vast market that is still undergoing industrialization and has a genuine demand for China. There are already many existing success stories: - Shein sells like hotcakes in Brazil, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia; - TikTok is really making money in Indonesia, Vietnam, and Brazil; - Transsion phones hold more than half of the market share in Africa and are known as the "King of Africa"; - ByteDance's Southeast Asian business contributes a significant amount of incremental revenue; Huawei's 5G and cloud businesses in the Middle East and Africa are crucial pillars after the blockade imposed by Europe and the United States; BYD, CATL, and DJI—their main battleground for globalization is not in the United States either. This is true globalization—based on real needs, comparative advantages, and mutual benefit, not on a mentality of "climbing the coattails of the strong." If you're working on AI, Middle Eastern sovereign wealth funds are pouring money into AI infrastructure; Southeast Asian countries are desperately seeking talent for localized large-scale models; the digital transformation of e-commerce, finance, and healthcare in Latin America is just beginning; and agricultural and educational AI in Africa has enormous social value potential. These opportunities are far more appealing than competing for valuations in the cutthroat Silicon Valley. In conclusion: When wolves come, we have shotguns. Currently, in the Manus case, the real victims are the company itself and its founders. But the real planners and promoters—the GPs, the consultants, the financial advisors—were almost completely unscathed. They placed the next order and did the same thing without hesitation. This is a very serious "structural flaw": If the instigators aren't punished, this won't stop. My specific suggestions are as follows: Establish a "List of Undesirable Investors." This should be modeled after the US Entity List, but with greater transparency and standardization. The standards must be clearly defined: for example, companies that systematically require investee companies to close their Chinese operations, assist in the transfer of core technical personnel, or include "anti-China" clauses in their investment agreements will be included in the list. The consequences of inclusion will include: restrictions on their development opportunities in China, restrictions on their partners' market access in China, and stricter scrutiny of the domestic operations of their invested companies. Intermediary institutions will also be held accountable. Law firms, investment banks, and financial advisors that assist with relocation structuring, Cayman Islands establishment, and cross-border tax planning should bear corresponding legal and administrative responsibilities if they knowingly provide services for transactions intended as "systematic relocation." This area is currently almost entirely unregulated. Precise constraints are needed on individual behavior. For example, GP partners who systematically plan and repeatedly manipulate similar transactions should be restricted from holding positions in domestic listed companies or state-owned funds. This isn't about overreaching, but rather about "holding individuals accountable for their actions"—linking personal choices to personal consequences. The key is to draw clear boundaries and focus on the issue, not the person. A US GP occasionally investing in a company that later expands overseas shouldn't be investigated; however, a GP manipulating "de-Sinicization" through over a dozen transactions over three years is a different matter. Open standards, transparent procedures, and the ability to appeal—this is what a mature policy should look like. Furthermore, based on the fundamental national policy of opening up to the outside world, a clear path should be provided for compliant foreign investment. The healthy development of China's AI startup ecosystem cannot be separated from diverse sources of capital. Therefore, policy design must be meticulous—distinguishing between "normal cross-border investment" and "systematic relocation operations." The former is welcomed, the latter is constrained. The judgment criteria do not consider the nationality of capital, but rather specific behaviors. Explore the "whitelist foreign funds" mechanism. Provide facilitation treatment to foreign institutions willing to comply with Chinese laws, committing to a certain percentage of their funds for domestic investment, and not participating in promoting the relocation of invested companies—including QFLP quotas, foreign exchange settlement, and tax incentives. Identify the "good students." Expand the QFLP and Cross-border Wealth Management Connect pilot programs. Provide compliant US dollar capital with legitimate channels for entry and exit, rather than forcing everyone to take gray-area routes. Keep dialogue channels open. Regularly hold roundtables with foreign funds to listen to their real demands and explain policy intentions. Many misunderstandings are caused by "information asymmetry + emotional dissemination," and most can be resolved by sitting down and talking. The core principle can be summarized in one sentence: What we oppose is using China as a springboard, not foreign investment itself. Only by clearly defining this line can China's entrepreneurial ecosystem maintain both its bottom line and its vitality. Returning to the question posed at the beginning of the article—how should we view the suspension of Manus? In short: a halt is necessary, but it's only the beginning. A halt is a signal to the market and capital—China does not accept the approach of using us as a stepping stone. This signal must be clear, powerful, and consistent. However, if it stops there, the halt becomes merely treating the symptoms, not the root cause. What's truly important is the systemic project that follows this signal: giving hope to those who stay in China to start businesses, making those who actively push for relocation pay the price, providing a clear channel for compliant foreign investment, and letting misled entrepreneurs hear the truth. None of these four things can be omitted. I am cautiously optimistic about this. China has experienced similar processes of "from prudence to inclusiveness" in many fields over the years—financial opening, data governance, and industrial policies. Each lesson learned is accompanied by growing pains, but ultimately makes us more stable. The AI ​​startup ecosystem is no exception. The Manus incident is not the end, the Miromind incident is not the end. The real end is when Chinese entrepreneurs no longer need to be pushed to go global, when dollar capital no longer considers "de-Sinicization" as the default option, and when "globalization" truly returns to its original meaning. On that day, nothing can stop China's path to building new-quality productive forces.
